All MoSMB-SA builds are set to use the "Standalone Mode" of operation by default. To use MoSMB server in Standalone Mode with Kerberos authentication simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one.

1. Configure Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on MoSMB server node. 2. Install Kerberos packages on mosmb server 3. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations.
Configure name resolution settings on MoSMB server.
Join the MoSMB server to the Active Directory Domain
Remove restricted Kerberos entries from Active Directory Domain
Configure DNS entries for the MoSMB Server
4. Kerberos and related services configurations on MoSMB server machine.
Configure MoSMB krb5 settings
Configure LDAP client settings
Configure SSSD settings
Configure MoSMB Kerberos keytab
Configure the domain_list.txt settings
For release versions:3.1.1_000 refer - MoSMB Kerberos Ticket Generation
For release versions:3.1.1_001 and above refer - MoSMB Kerberos Ticket Generation.
5. MoSMB server Now supports NTLMSSP authentication from Release versions:3.1.1_004 and above. 6. Configure required authorization (ACL) settings
Using MoSMB with Posix ACL OR
Using MoSMB with Advance Posix ACL OR
Using MoSMB with NTACL
7. Setup the MoSMB configuration file (/etc/mosmb/mosmb.json) for standalone use.
In globalSection set enableHighAvailability to no
In section authSection , set authMode as krb5
In section nodeInfoSection , give any integer between 1 to 1023 to nodeId
In section vinodeSection set mechanism as inode
In shareSection set parameter isCluster to no
Example:
8. Set Priority List for AD IPs (in a AD Farm) if required. If you have a AD farm, and want mosmb to use only few of them for any reason:
Add Priority List for AD IPs in /etc/mosmb
9. If the server machines are centos(or similar) 8 and above supported encryption options needs to be enabled from server side as well in order for Kerberos authentication to work completely.

On every CentOS 8.3 and above MoSMB server, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place, use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

Command : update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Output : Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Command : init 6 Notes : 1. System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up so it is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place.
           3. The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on CentOS 8.3 and newer.

To enable support for RC4 in CentOS 8.2 , create and enable a custom cryptographic module policy with cipher = RC4-128+. For more details, see Customizing system-wide cryptographic policies with policy modifiers. Reference links :
1. Using the system wide cryptographic policies 2. how to customize crypto policies rhel 8.2
To enable support for RC4 in CentOS 8.0 and CentOS 8.1 , add +rc4 to the permitted_enctypes option in the /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/krb5.config file

[libdefaults]
           permitted_enctypes = aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192 camellia256-cts-cmac aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128 camellia128-cts-cmac +rc4
10. If the server machines have openssl version 3 and above configuration changes are required.
Open /etc/ssl/openssl.cnf
Existing/Default Configuration :

[provider_sect]
   default = default_sect
   [default_sect]
   # activate = 1
Edits/Required Configuration :

[provider_sect]
   default = default_sect legacy = legacy_sect [openssl_init]
   providers = provider_sect
 
   [default_sect] activate = 1 [legacy_sect] activate = 1
11. Start MoSMB service and check status service mosmb status and check the mosmb.log for details. You can check details of MoSMB server Maintenance here
Notes :

1. Make sure sssd service is running on node.  
   2. Node id can be any integer in the 1 to 1023 range.
   3. Parameters other than mechanism is skipped from vinodeSection in case of MoSMB-SA build. 
   4. To know other mosmb.json parameters in details refer this .

NTACL is the Windows authorization mechanism; therefore it is supported only with Kerberos authentication. In order to enable NTACL support for a MoSMB share which is newly created path or any old path which does not have any extended attributes, follow bellow steps:
1. MoSMB configurations : /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json
In shareSection under aclStrategy set parameter acl to ntacl
In shareSection set parameter metadataStore to xattr
Example:
2. Setting / Adding ACEs and Owner from Windows client Follow these steps once complete Kerberos setup is carried out and share is accessible with FQDN of server/cluster.
1. Access server from client and right click on the share for which you want to set the permissions and Open share Properties → Security
2. Initially there are no owner set to the share. To check owner click on Advanced settings.
3. To add an owner for the share, click on "Owner" : "Change"
4. To add ACEs , click on "Edit" and select "Add" to add your user to the share from share properties window.
5. Set User permissions for the added User and Click on "Apply" .
6. Click on "Yes" and then "Apply" permissions.
7. Follow the same steps to add more users/groups.
Note: Rename and delete operations with NTACLs work when modify permission is given to the file/directory while setting permissions.
3. Inheritance in ACEs If the share has existing files/folders inside it before acl settings are configured or change the configuration to "ntacl" please check the following settings:
1. Right click on share. Open Properties → Security → Advanced → Add
2. Click on Select a principal and add a user to the share as specified in step 2 .5
3. Ensure that  the “Applies to” option is set to “this folder, subfolders and files”.
4. Initially, if the Owner is not set for the share, the logged in user of the client is automatically set as the owner of the share as well as folders and sub folders.
5. Inheritance: Enable inheritance while assigning permissions to the share or a folder by clicking on Properties → Security → Advanced → Enable Inheritance .
Note: Time taken to apply inherited permissions will be dependent on the number of files/folders in the share.
Notes :

1. Initially no acls are applied to the share contents and the user cannot see the owner. The share is not accessible to any user/group until ACEs are applied to the share.
   2. Rename and delete operations with NTACLs work when modify permission is given to the file/directory while setting permissions.
   3. Currently “This folder, subfolder and files” option is supported only for “Applied to:” field.
   4. Time taken to apply inherited permissions will be dependent on the number of files/folders in the share.
Contents
1 Start MoSMB-ZooKeeper
2 Check MoSMB-ZooKeeper status
3 Stop MoSMB-ZooKeeper
4 Cleanup MoSMB-ZooKeeper
Start MoSMB-ZooKeeper
Go in MoSMB-ZooKeeper directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin and run the following command:
./bin/zkServer.sh start
This will create a MoSMB-ZooKeeper log file at location ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/ that will contain the MoSMB-ZooKeeper server related logs.
Check MoSMB-ZooKeeper status
Go in MoSMB-ZooKeeper directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin and run the following command:
Command   : ./bin/zkServer.sh status Output    : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg' Mode: Leader
Mode: Leader / Follower / Standalone this output will be different on individual servers depending on the MoSMB-ZooKeeper configurations
Stop MoSMB-ZooKeeper
Go in MoSMB-ZooKeeper directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin and run the following command:
./bin/zkServer.sh stop
Cleanup MoSMB-ZooKeeper

MoSMB provides the zookeeper server cleanup script for local zookeeper nodes. Notes : 1. Script should run when MoSMB server is not running. 2. This script can be used only for the local zookeeper server. 3. When zookeeper is not running, this script can not remove the nodes.
Name:

[Older Releases] -- /etc/mosmb/scripts/zk-cleanup.sh
   [Newer Releases] --  /etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh
Description:

This script is used to cleanup the zookeeper server node/s. It will remove all the nodes except for the /mosmb_s3_root and zookeeper’s own nodes. When and if MoSMB nodes inside zookeeper are directed under special “chrootsuffix” then only the nodes under configured path are removed except for the /mosmb_s3_root and zookeeper’s own nodes.
Usage:

Command: zk-cleanup.sh -h OR mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh -h zk-cleanup.sh <zookeeper_dir_path>     OR  mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh <zookeeper_dir_path>
   
   Here, zookeeper_dir_path is the path where MoSMB-Zookeeper in installed.
Example:

Command: /etc/mosmb/scripts/zk-cleanup.sh /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin Output: dataDir in /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg is dataDir=/var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb .... Cleaned zookeeper files

Download MoSMB-CL Package

Extract Package

MoSMB-ZooKeeper Installation

1. One EDF node should have the clustershell configured for executing commands in parallel on a cluster. 2. Identify the nodes on which MoSMB-ZooKeeper would be installed (add the group to clustershell (clush) configuration so that it can be used for MoSMB-ZooKeeper installation configuration and management.) 3. Install the mosmb-zookeeper rpm on all zk nodes
clush -g zk rpm -ivh /tmp/mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1.x86_64.rpm
For more details refer: Installing zookeeper for MoSMB on CentOS/RHEL
Configure the mosmb-zookeeper cluster
Copy reference mosmb_zoo.cfg to zoo.cfg
cp /etc/mosmb/mosmb_zoo.cfg /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg
In this zoo.cfg check/update
globalOutstandingLimit =1000000
forceSync =no
dataDir =<update the dedicated zookeeper data folder>
Update all the zookeeper node entries under Server List in the zoo.cfg
Copy the zoo.cfg to all zookeeper nodes in /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache<version>/conf folder
clush -B -g zk -c /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg
Create data directory if not present on all mosmb-zookeeper nodes
Create myid files in the data directory of each mosmb-zookeeper node with same id as mentioned in zoo.cfg server list
For more details refer: mosmb-Zookeeper cluster configuration
Setup zookeeper server side log rotation if desired

This step is not mandatory. In case zookeeper restarts, the server side log located in the log folder of zookeeper installation path, (/opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/) is overwritten and old logs are over-written/lost. To avoid loss of old logs, one can do the following
1. A solution is to modify zkEnv.sh and set ZOO_LOG4J_PROP

$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP
if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ]
 ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE"
2. here add ROLLINGFILE

$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP
if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ]
  ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE, ROLLINGFILE "
3. Now every restart of mosmb-zookeeper service will have two log files, one current (.out) and one backed up with old logs (.log).
MoSMB rpm Installation
Install mosmb rpm on all EDF nodes
clush -a sudo rpm -ivh /tmp/MoSMB-CL-3.1.1_038-20230323_0.centos-8.x86_64.rpm
Check the mosmb package details after rpm installation
clush -ab sudo mosmb -b
sssd configuration
sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users.
If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on the MoSMB server machine.
Make sure the sssd configuration file /etc/sssd/sssd.conf contains information of all the domains MoSMB server is part of with parameters as shown below.
Comma (,) separated  list of all domains should be metioned
All Domains should  have its own section for detail configuration settings.

[sssd] domains = mosmbad.dom, mosmbad2.dom config_file_version = 2
   services = nss, pam [domain/mosmbad.dom] ad_domain = mosmbad.dom
   krb5_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM
   realmd_tags = manages-system joined-with-adcli
   cache_credentials = True
   id_provider = ad
   krb5_store_password_if_offline = True
   default_shell = /bin/bash ldap_id_mapping = True use_fully_qualified_names = True
   fallback_homedir = /home/%u@%d
   access_provider = ad [domain/mosmbad2.dom] ad_domain = mosmbad2.dom
   krb5_realm = MOSMBAD2.DOM
   realmd_tags = manages-system joined-with-adcli
   cache_credentials = True
   id_provider = ad
   krb5_store_password_if_offline = True
   default_shell = /bin/bash ldap_id_mapping = True use_fully_qualified_names = True
   fallback_homedir = /home/%u@%d
   access_provider = ad
sssd configuration files must be at /etc/sssd/ with permissions of files set to 600 (-rw-------)
Start sssd service with service sssd start OR systemctl start sssd .
nsswitch.conf Configuration
Verify that nsswitch.conf file is configured to use SSSD as a provider
Open /etc/nsswitch.conf file and verify that it points to sss for the name service switch

passwd:     files sss
   shadow:     files sss
   group:      files sss
   netgroup:   files sss
Note :
Restart the sssd service after making any changes in the sssd configurations.
Important : Kindly complete the Network Configuration Settings before you configure Kerberos on the MoSMB server.

Installing Kerberos

The MoSMB server authenticates the AD Domain users using Kerberos authentication method. This requires a Kerberos ticket granted to the server by configured AD machine. This ticket generation can be done using the kinit commands.
On CentOS
Search and install the Kerberos package on CentOS:
Example:
Command 1 : yum search kerberos Output 1  : .... krb5-workstation.x86_64 : Kerberos 5 programs for use on workstations Command 2 : yum install krb5-workstation.x86_64
On Ubuntu
Search and install the Kerberos package on Ubuntu:
Example:
Command 1 : apt-cache search krb5 Output 1  : .... krb5-user - basic programs to authenticate using MIT Kerberos Command 2 : apt-get install krb5-user
krb5.conf
Make sure these entries are present in the /etc/krb5.conf file.
Include directory - /etc/krb5.conf.d
Modify libdefaults section - rdns, default_ccache_name entries are present refer - cache type limitations
Example:
includedir /etc/krb5.conf.d/ [libdefaults] .......... rdns = false default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt ..........
Create and open a new file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
Add realms section - kdc and admin_server entries.
Add domain_realm section - domain name entry is present.
Example:
[realms] MOSMBAD.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom master_kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom } [domain_realm] mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM .mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM
Limitations for cache type
with kerberos 1.13 and above  MoSMB support cache types FILE, KCM, DIR. keyring is not supported.

# cat /etc/krb5.conf | grep default_ccache_name
       default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/mosmb_kerberos_ticket OR default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_ticket_dir #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid}      [not supported] # cat /etc/krb5.conf.d/kcm_default_ccache | grep default_ccache_name
       default_ccache_name = KCM:
Notes :
MoSMB will support only cache type FILE for kerberos versions below 1.13
The FILE and DIR path can be of your choice. Make sure path is anything other than /tmp for persistence
mosmb.keytab
Standalone Server MoSMB Keytab generation
The MoSMB keytab location is /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab

Assumption is domain join is carried out successfully and there are no restricted entries for kerberos on configured AD. If restricted entries are present on AD side please go through Removing restricted Kerberos entries
Run the ktutil to Read and add existing host keys to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab from krb5.keytab and then add required entries.
Run the ktutil commands as follows:

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Example:

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Notes :
The domain user used in above commands and the user used to generate the ticket (as explained in next few steps using kinit) should be the same.
The domain name in above commands must be in uppercase.
If and when the user password or user spn is changed Kerberos keytab related settings are needed to carry out again
MoSMB Cluster Keytab generation
Refer to keytab generation steps in section Cluster Keytab Generation
Removing restricted Kerberos entries

When there are restricted Kerberos entries on the server machine seen as output of command klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab Check if they are also present on the AD side if present we need to remove them from AD,
Login to AD machine with user who has admin rights and open Active directory administrative Center
Open computers section
In computers open properties of the MoSMB server machine
In Properties of server machine  → Extensions → Attribute Editor
In Attribute editor select the attribute "servicePrincipalName"
Remove restricted entries from the servicePrincipalName against the MoSMB server.
Example -

restrictedkrbhost/moserver1.mosmbad.dom
   restrictedkrbhost/MOSERVER1
Note :
After making changes on AD machine destroy the previously taken Kerberos tickets if any from server machine with kdestroy -A and generate new ticket.
Generating a Kerberos Ticket (Releases before March 2022)
To generate a ticket on the server use the kinit -V <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> command. The command asks password for the specified user.
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -V mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Output    : Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

OR To generate a ticket on the server without having to use the password, use command kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> . -k Specifies to obtain the key for the ticket principal from a key table. So you are not prompted to enter the password for the ticket principal, and -t will refer to the keytab file specified
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Note :
The domain name in above commands must be in UPPERCASE to avoid KDC mismatch issues while getting credentials.
Also check the ticket time with klist -a, (time of MoSMB server and AD should be always synced or Kerberos authentication may fail)
Generating Kerberos Ticket (Releases after March 2022)

In new releases, user will NOT be needed to acquire a ticket manually. /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries will be in the format of user@DOMAIN / cifs/user.domain@DOMAIN for standalone server, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive . To generate a ticket on the server, MoSMB will use principal name from domain_list.txt, match against the one present in mosmb.keytab and use it to generate the kerberos ticket.
Example for STANDALONE Server

# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM # klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
   KVNO Principal---- --------------------------------------------
    .
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Example for CLUSTER
# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM

# klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
    KVNO Principal---- -------------------------------------------- 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
Verifying a Kerberos Ticket
To verify if the ticket is acquired for the domain_user, run the klist command.
Example:

Command : sudo klist -A Output  : Ticket cache: FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt Default principal: mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Valid starting       Expires              Service principal 01/16/2020 11:33:50  01/16/2020 21:33:50  krbtgt/MOSMBAD.DOM@MOSMBAD.DOM renew until 01/17/2020 11:33:45
Note :
By default, the Kerberos ticket is valid for 10 hours .
The Kerberos ticket defaults may be modified by the Group Policy Management → Kerberos Policy → Security Settings in the AD.
Muti-Domain kerberos configuration
Carry out following changes in /etc/krb5.conf and /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf

Set default cache path to a directory location other than /tmp directory. DIR / KCM cache type is used in case of the multiple Kerberos tickets. default_ccache_name = DIR:<directory path> OR default_ccache_name = KCM: in /etc/krb5.conf
Example :
[libdefaults] #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid} default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/Kerberos_tickets default_realm = MOSMBAD1.DOM
Mention default realm as the domain to which server machine is joined in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
In the realms section mention all the realms in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
Example :
[realms] MOSMBAD1.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom admin_server = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom } MOSMBAD2.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom admin_server = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom }

Note: In case of common global name space across sites. 1. Add only local keytab on the server side. 2. For sssd configuration to work MoSMB servers must be part of all domains. 3. Generation of only local Kerberos ticket would be sufficient. (instead of DIR cache path in /etc/krb5.conf mention FILE path and file name)
LDAP Client Configurations
To enable the  ACL view on the client side you are required to perform the following operations, in case of Kerberos authentication:
A. Add a BASE entry in the LDAP configuration file /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) . The format should be as follows:
BASE   dc=example,dc=com
Example:
BASE    dc=mosmbad,dc=dom
B. Add the entry for the LDAP URI OR HOST to the /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) file. The format should be as follows:

URI    ldap://<FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
   OR
   HOST    <FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
Example :
URI    ldap://mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom OR HOST    mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom
Note :
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the LDAP related configuration, similar to above examples.
sssd Configuration

sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users.
After carrying out the domain join of the MoSMB server make sure that the sssd configurations are in place.
If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on the server machine.
Create a sssd configuration file /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf and it should contain parameters as shown in image below.
sssd configuration file location must be at /etc/sssd/sssd.conf with permissions of file set to 600 (-rw-------) and same with /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf
Start sssd service with service sssd start .
Note :
Restart the sssd service after making the sssd configurations as in the above image.
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the sssd related configuration, similar to above example.
Make entries of all domains MoSMB server is joined to as explained below.
domain_list.txt Configuration for sssd

For sssd related settings simply create a file named domain_list.txt at the /etc/mosmb/ location and mention the domain to which the MoSMB server is joined. Ensure that you make an entry for each domain on a new line.
Example:
#cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosmbad.dom

For sssd related settings simply create a file named /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of just domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosuer@MOSMBAD.DOM
In new releases, /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
nsswitch.conf Configuration

Verify that nsswitch.conf file is configured to use SSSD as a provider
Open /etc/nsswitch.conf file and verify that it points to sss for the name service switch

passwd:     files sss
shadow:     files sss
group:      files sss
netgroup:   files sss
Configure the MoSMB-ZooKeeper Server
1. Copy sample ZooKeeper configuration file from /etc/mosmb/ to zookeeper installation conf folder.
Example :
cp /etc/mosmb/mosmb_zoo.cfg /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg
2. Create ZooKeeper server data directory. It can be any preferred location other than /tmp.
Example :
mkdir -p /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb
3. MoSMB-ZooKeeper cluster configuration file should look as follows: configuration file location - </path/to/>apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg

# The number of milliseconds of each tick tickTime=50000 # The number of ticks that the initial # synchronization phase can take initLimit=50 # The number of ticks that can pass between # sending a request and getting an acknowledgement #syncEnabled=false forceSync=no
   syncLimit=20 #To prevent ZooKeeper from running out of memory due to queued requests, #ZooKeeper will throttle clients so that there is no more than globalOutstandingLimit #outstanding requests in the system. The default limit is 1,000. globalOutstandingLimit=10000 #Controls the number of requests that will be queued server-side to be processed by the ZooKeeper server. #Connections that exceed this length will receive a network timeout (30s) which may cause ZooKeeper #session expiry issues. By default, this value is unset (-1) which, on Linux, uses a backlog of 50. clientPortListenBacklog=500 #maxbuffer specifies the maximum size of the data that can be stored in a znode. The default is 0xfffff, or just #under 1M. If this option is changed, the system property must be set on all servers and clients otherwise problems will arise. #mosmb is setting it to 8k jute.maxbuffer=0x2000 #maxClientCnxns=0 # the directory where the snapshot is stored. # do not use /tmp for storage, /tmp here is just # example sakes. dataDir=/var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb # the port at which the clients will connect clientPort=2182 #4lw.commands.whitelist=* admin.serverPort=8082 # Be sure to read the maintenance section of the # administrator guide before turning on autopurge. # http://zookeeper.apache.org/doc/current/zookeeperAdmin.html #sc_maintenance # The number of snapshots to retain in dataDir autopurge.snapRetainCount=3 # Purge task interval in hours # Set to "0" to disable auto purge feature autopurge.purgeInterval=1 #server list #server.1=<zookeeper node1 ip>:4888:5888 server.1=13.12.12.147:4888:5888
   server.2=13.12.12.148:4888:5888
   server.3=13.12.12.149:4888:5888
For detailed information on the configuration parameters refer to the ZooKeeper Server Configuration Parameters page.

4. Create the myid file on all the MoSMB-ZooKeeper servers in zookeeper cluster at the path mentioned as dataDir in </path/to/>apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg In above example file it is - /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/ .
Example:

Test data                      : ZooKeeper Server IP list - 13.12.12.147, 13.12.12.148, 13.12.12.149 File on server 1 (13.12.12.147)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 1 (13.12.12.147) : 1 File on server 2 (13.12.12.148)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 2 (13.12.12.148) : 2 File on server 3 (13.12.12.149)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 3 (13.12.12.149) : 3
MoSMB - mosmb.json configurations for MoSMB-ZooKeeper cluster server
In vinodeSection of /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file is to be edited as per the MoSMB-ZooKeeper cluster settings in zoo.cfg
Notes :

1. Any changes in zoo.cfg mandates restart of zookeeper service on all nodes.
   2. Any changes in mosmb.json mandates a restart on mosmb service on all nodes
   3. MoSMB-ZooKeeper is a centralized service for maintaining configuration information, naming, providing distributed synchronization, and providing group services.
Important : Kindly complete the Network Configuration Settings before you configure Kerberos on the MoSMB server.

Standalone Server MoSMB Keytab generation
The MoSMB keytab location is /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab

Assumption is domain join is carried out successfully and there are no restricted entries for kerberos on configured AD. If restricted entries are present on AD side please go through Removing restricted Kerberos entries
Run the ktutil to Read and add existing host keys to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab from krb5.keytab and then add required entries.

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Example:

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Notes :
The domain user used in above commands and the user used to generate the ticket (as explained in next few steps using kinit) should be the same.
The domain name in above commands must be in uppercase.
If and when the user password or user spn is changed Kerberos keytab related settings are needed to carry out again
MoSMB Cluster Keytab generation
Refer to keytab generation steps in section Cluster Keytab Generation
Removing restricted Kerberos entries

When there are restricted Kerberos entries on the server machine seen as output of command klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab Check if they are also present on the AD side if present we need to remove them from AD,
Login to AD machine with user who has admin rights and open Active directory administrative Center
Open computers section
In computers open properties of the MoSMB server machine
In Properties of server machine  → Extensions → Attribute Editor
In Attribute editor select the attribute "servicePrincipalName"
Remove restricted entries from the servicePrincipalName against the MoSMB server.
Example -

restrictedkrbhost/moserver1.mosmbad.dom
   restrictedkrbhost/MOSERVER1
Note :
After making changes on AD machine destroy the previously taken Kerberos tickets if any from server machine with kdestroy -A and generate new ticket.
Generating a Kerberos Ticket (Releases before March 2022)
To generate a ticket on the server use the kinit -V <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> command. The command asks password for the specified user.
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -V mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Output    : Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

OR To generate a ticket on the server without having to use the password, use command kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> . -k Specifies to obtain the key for the ticket principal from a key table. So you are not prompted to enter the password for the ticket principal, and -t will refer to the keytab file specified
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Note :
The domain name in above commands must be in UPPERCASE to avoid KDC mismatch issues while getting credentials.
Also check the ticket time with klist -a, (time of MoSMB server and AD should be always synced or Kerberos authentication may fail)
Generating Kerberos Ticket (Releases after March 2022)

In new releases, user will NOT be needed to acquire a ticket manually. /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries will be in the format of user@DOMAIN / cifs/user.domain@DOMAIN for standalone server, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive . To generate a ticket on the server, MoSMB will use principal name from domain_list.txt, match against the one present in mosmb.keytab and use it to generate the kerberos ticket.
Example for STANDALONE Server

# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM # klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
   KVNO Principal---- --------------------------------------------
    .
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Example for CLUSTER
# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM

# klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
    KVNO Principal---- -------------------------------------------- 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
Verifying a Kerberos Ticket
To verify if the ticket is acquired for the domain_user, run the klist command.
Example:

Command : sudo klist -A Output  : Ticket cache: FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt Default principal: mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Valid starting       Expires              Service principal 01/16/2020 11:33:50  01/16/2020 21:33:50  krbtgt/MOSMBAD.DOM@MOSMBAD.DOM renew until 01/17/2020 11:33:45
Note :
By default, the Kerberos ticket is valid for 10 hours .
The Kerberos ticket defaults may be modified by the Group Policy Management → Kerberos Policy → Security Settings in the AD.
Muti-Domain kerberos configuration
Carry out following changes in /etc/krb5.conf and /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf

Set default cache path to a directory location other than /tmp directory. DIR / KCM cache type is used in case of the multiple Kerberos tickets. default_ccache_name = DIR:<directory path> OR default_ccache_name = KCM: in /etc/krb5.conf
Example :
[libdefaults] #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid} default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/Kerberos_tickets default_realm = MOSMBAD1.DOM
Mention default realm as the domain to which server machine is joined in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
In the realms section mention all the realms in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
Example :
[realms] MOSMBAD1.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom admin_server = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom } MOSMBAD2.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom admin_server = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom }

Note: In case of common global name space across sites. 1. Add only local keytab on the server side. 2. For sssd configuration to work MoSMB servers must be part of all domains. 3. Generation of only local Kerberos ticket would be sufficient. (instead of DIR cache path in /etc/krb5.conf mention FILE path and file name)
LDAP Client Configurations
To enable the  ACL view on the client side you are required to perform the following operations, in case of Kerberos authentication:
A. Add a BASE entry in the LDAP configuration file /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) . The format should be as follows:
BASE   dc=example,dc=com
Example:
BASE    dc=mosmbad,dc=dom
B. Add the entry for the LDAP URI OR HOST to the /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) file. The format should be as follows:

URI    ldap://<FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
   OR
   HOST    <FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
Example :
URI    ldap://mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom OR HOST    mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom
Note :
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the LDAP related configuration, similar to above examples.
sssd Configuration

sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users.

If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on the server machine.
Create a sssd configuration file /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf and it should contain parameters as shown in image below.
sssd configuration file location must be at /etc/sssd/sssd.conf with permissions of file set to 600 (-rw-------) and same with /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf
Start sssd service with service sssd start .
Note :
Restart the sssd service after making the sssd configurations as in the above image.
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the sssd related configuration, similar to above example.
Make entries of all domains MoSMB server is joined to as explained below.
domain_list.txt Configuration for sssd

For sssd related settings simply create a file named domain_list.txt at the /etc/mosmb/ location and mention the domain to which the MoSMB server is joined. Ensure that you make an entry for each domain on a new line.

#cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosmbad.dom

For sssd related settings simply create a file named /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of just domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosuer@MOSMBAD.DOM
In new releases, /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
nsswitch.conf Configuration

Verify that nsswitch.conf file is configured to use SSSD as a provider
Open /etc/nsswitch.conf file and verify that it points to sss for the name service switch

passwd:     files sss

This is an introduction to MoSMB to get you started with the MoSMB server on any server platform like CentOS, Ubuntu, and Debian.

MoSMB is a proprietary ANSI C SMB v3 Server implementation in user space only and supported on major Linux flavors. It is designed to be fully compliant with Microsoft Server Message Block (SMB) version 3 Protocol specifications. It is designed to be a high performance, scalable, highly available, user-space server implementation that has an API for third-party integration.
The entire MoSMB configuration can be categorized into seven major categories, namely:
Server Modes
Authentication Modes
Authorization Modes
Networking (DNS & NTP)
MoSMB License settings
Share Configuration , and
Maintenance
MoSMB Features
The following MoSMB features are available in the various MoSMB versions - Standard, Advance and Media.
Major Features :
Microsoft SMB 2.* and 3.0.* Protocol support
Support for VFS filesystems (Ext4, XFS, ZFS, GlusterFS, LustreFS, and so on)
Active Directory Integration and Kerberos support
NTLMSSP (NTLM-AD) support
NTLM local support
Signing with HMAC-SHA256, AES-CMAC, AES-GMAC ciphers
Encryption with AES-128-CCM, AES-128-GCM, AES-256-CCM, AES-256-GCM ciphers
SMB Direct (RDMA) support
RDMA data transformation using the above mentioned signing and encryption ciphers
Basic Posix ACLs support
Basic Windows ACLs (DACLs only)
Advance Posix ACL (using extended attributes)
SMB multi-channel
Persistent Handle v1 and v2
SMB open file modes, SMB locks supported across cluster nodes
Active-Passive cluster support
Active-Active Scale-out cluster support
Highly Salable as per system resources available (no of connections, no of sessions, no of simultaneous file opens etc.)
Transparent Failover and High Availability support
Distributed MoSMB file shares as Distributed File System
SMB clients supported – Windows, Mac, Linux
High Performance (iops/latency/throughput)
Server Side Copy Operation Support
Direct I/O support for RDMA (and TCP)
Low Resource Usage (CPU/memory/etc)
MoSMB supports S3 Object Store interface (AddOn: S3 plugin)
S3 driver plug-in for Object Stores like AWS, Minio, and so on
Compatible with S3 browsers
Basic MMC support for management
Config CLI support for MoSMB configuration (mosmbcli)
Microsoft SMB 3.1.1 Protocol Compliance
Performance Metrices are available
MoSMB metrices Integration support available for monitoring tools like Checkmk, Prometheus
Multi domain, multi forest support
File and directory Leasing
Named Streams
AAPL Context
Ansible scripts for installation and upgrade
Witness Server
Stale file handling and scavenging
SMB Tree Connect Redirection (Load Balancing using storage node affinity)
Minor Features :
Provisioning of shares at runtime
Unicode support
DFS shares Compatible
Heterogeneous storage support at share level
Support for LDAPS communication
Upcoming Features :
NFSv4 ACL support
S3 Bucket Notifications
Supported Use Cases
MS SQL 2012 and above databases hosted on the MoSMB SMB3 shares.
Hyper-V (managed by SCVMM). VM stored on the MoSMB SMB3 shares. Use cases such as VM Cloning, Template, Live Migration, and so on.
High performance File Server for User Home Directory and Media Streaming.
Continuously Available (CA) for Client Applications using the Witness Server.
System Requirements
and Network Time Protocol Service (NTP), LDAP, SSSD
Port List for MoSMB
The following is a comprehensive list of port numbers used by the MoSMB server and MoSMB related miscellaneous services:

From version 091 onwards, MoSMB will work with LDAPS. MoSMB will attempt Secure LDAP (LDAP over SSL/TLS) communication to the AD server (to port 636) and if it is not setup then will fallback to non secure LDAP communication over port 389. The port 636 communication setup to AD can be checked with command openssl s_client -connect <AD_IP>:636
Supported SMB Clients
Multi-protocol Caveat

MoSMB does NOT recommend and support concurrent multiprotocol write access of the same file or directory via SMB and NFS/native posix client/backend access. This is because mosmb supports distributed SMB locking, byte-range locks, file sharing modes etc. and other protocols do not. Concurrent access over other protocols (or natively) and modifying files/folders without unified locking can cause data inconsistency for Windows applications that are accessing over SMB (mosmb).

Background and Recommendation In an enterprise environment, it is common that Linux, Windows and macOS workloads require access to the same data. It is recommended that multiprotocol access NOT be used for this purpose. Instead, either MoSMB or NFS, but not both should be used to access a particular file. This is because MoSMB and NFS use different locking mechanisms and using both to access a file concurrently can lead to data inconsistency issues. That is why, for example, Amazon EFS, Amazon FSx, or Azure Files, provide access to files via either NFS or SMB, not both. Since it is not possible to control access at the granularity of a single file or directory, it is recommended that data should be partitioned at the volume level so that each volume is accessed via NFS or MoSMB, but not both. MoSMB supports access to all 3- Linux, Windows and macOS clients in a consistent manner, so there should be no data consistency issue when these different OS clients access the same file or directory concurrently.

The above data consistency issue can also occur when accessing files concurrently via MoSMB and natively using the POSIX client. It is recommended that for such access also only one of the access mechanisms be used for a particular volume and not both.
Support for Priority List of IPs in an AD farm
When you have a AD farm, and want mosmb to use only few of them for any reason, this can be achieved using a Priority list. Create a file in /etc/mosmb/ as

ex: 
 cat /etc/mosmb/TESTDOMAIN.DOM_whitelist
 10.12.0.20

1.1 Enable SMB Direct in mosmb.json
1.2 SMB Direct ring size
1.3 SMB Direct buffer size
1.4 TCP parameters
RDMA (smbDirect)
Note : Refer to the Troubleshooting Guide for the RDMA (SMBDirect) Communication on a MoSMB Server to understand how to make the RDMA (SMBDirect) basic setup work.

The SMBDirect protocol uses the RDMA Read/Write requests to transfer file data within the client and the server. Every RDMA Read/Write operation consists of a SMB2 Read request with 1 or more buffer descriptors . Each buffer descriptor contains information for an I/O buffer with the desired transfer size. Each buffer descriptor thus requires to be allocated and pinned in memory till the transfer is completed through the RDMA channel. So for every buffer descriptor a buffer of desired size is allocated and pinned, this is a control operation and consumes a significant amount of time. Therefore we have introduced the following parameters to ensure that we reduce control operations during data operations:
Enable SMB Direct in mosmb.json
Edit the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file , and set enableSmbDirect parameter value to yes
SMB Direct ring size
Default value: 512
Per connection based parameter.
Accepts value in the multiples of 64.

The server should be restarted to reflect the effect when a  parameter is changed. The SMB Direct ring size is a parameter that allocates and reserves the specified number of buffers per RDMA connection, each of the SMBDirect buffer size size. On every RDMA Read/Write request the buffer is consumed from this ring and replenished after use. This allows faster operations as we can avoid memory pinning operations (control) in the data path resulting in faster speeds. This can to be tuned w.r.t the I/O pattern for typical application. Setting it to very higher number can guarantee faster transfers at the cost of higher memory footprint. Whereas setting it to very lower number can lead to slower speeds due to the lack of available buffers for transfers. We recommend it to be set 1024 for media applications, and 256 for non-media operations (basic file server, disk io apps)
SMB Direct buffer size
Default value: 1
Per connection-based parameter.
Accepts value from 1 to 8 inclusive. This value is in Mega Bytes (for example 1 represents 1MB).

The server should be restarted to reflect the effect of a changed parameter. This parameter represents the size of transfer for every buffer descriptor from SMB Direct ring . As per the SMBD Specification this parameter should be less than or equal to the maximum fragmented receive size (1MB). However, this parameter could be less than or equal to the maximum transaction size (8MB) as well. The SMB client has 1MB value  that is the maximum fragmented receive size and hence it the recommended value.
TCP parameters
For RDMA connections only, allocate minimal resources to TCP parameters since the TCP buffers will consume some amount of RAM
For example, preProcessorThreads = 4, ringBufferCount = 256 (or 128 or 64)

Installing Kerberos

The MoSMB server authenticates the AD Domain users using Kerberos authentication method. This requires a Kerberos ticket granted to the server by configured AD machine. This ticket generation can be done using the kinit commands.
On CentOS
Search and install the Kerberos package on CentOS:
Example:
Command 1 : yum search kerberos Output 1  : .... krb5-workstation.x86_64 : Kerberos 5 programs for use on workstations Command 2 : yum install krb5-workstation.x86_64
On Ubuntu
Search and install the Kerberos package on Ubuntu:
Example:
Command 1 : apt-cache search krb5 Output 1  : .... krb5-user - basic programs to authenticate using MIT Kerberos Command 2 : apt-get install krb5-user
krb5.conf
Make sure these entries are present in the /etc/krb5.conf file.
Include directory - /etc/krb5.conf.d
Modify libdefaults section - rdns, default_ccache_name entries are present refer - cache type limitations
Example:
includedir /etc/krb5.conf.d/ [libdefaults] .......... rdns = false default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt ..........
Create and open a new file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
Add realms section - kdc and admin_server entries.
Add domain_realm section - domain name entry is present.
Example:
[realms] MOSMBAD.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom master_kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom } [domain_realm] mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM .mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM
Limitations for cache type
with kerberos 1.13 and above  MoSMB support cache types FILE, KCM, DIR. keyring is not supported.

# cat /etc/krb5.conf | grep default_ccache_name
       default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/mosmb_kerberos_ticket OR default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_ticket_dir #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid}      [not supported] # cat /etc/krb5.conf.d/kcm_default_ccache | grep default_ccache_name
       default_ccache_name = KCM:
Notes :
MoSMB will support only cache type FILE for kerberos versions below 1.13
The FILE and DIR path can be of your choice. Make sure path is anything other than /tmp for persistence
mosmb.keytab
Standalone Server MoSMB Keytab generation
The MoSMB keytab location is /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab

Assumption is domain join is carried out successfully and there are no restricted entries for kerberos on configured AD. If restricted entries are present on AD side please go through Removing restricted Kerberos entries
Run the ktutil to Read and add existing host keys to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab from krb5.keytab and then add required entries.
Run the ktutil commands as follows:

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Example:

ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab
   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

   ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM: 
   
   ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
Notes :
The domain user used in above commands and the user used to generate the ticket (as explained in next few steps using kinit) should be the same.
The domain name in above commands must be in uppercase.
If and when the user password or user spn is changed Kerberos keytab related settings are needed to carry out again
MoSMB Cluster Keytab generation
Refer to keytab generation steps in section Cluster Keytab Generation
Removing restricted Kerberos entries

When there are restricted Kerberos entries on the server machine seen as output of command klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab Check if they are also present on the AD side if present we need to remove them from AD,
Login to AD machine with user who has admin rights and open Active directory administrative Center
Open computers section
In computers open properties of the MoSMB server machine
In Properties of server machine  → Extensions → Attribute Editor
In Attribute editor select the attribute "servicePrincipalName"
Remove restricted entries from the servicePrincipalName against the MoSMB server.
Example -

restrictedkrbhost/moserver1.mosmbad.dom
   restrictedkrbhost/MOSERVER1
Note :
After making changes on AD machine destroy the previously taken Kerberos tickets if any from server machine with kdestroy -A and generate new ticket.
Generating a Kerberos Ticket (Releases before March 2022)
To generate a ticket on the server use the kinit -V <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> command. The command asks password for the specified user.
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -V mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Output    : Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM:

OR To generate a ticket on the server without having to use the password, use command kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab <AD_Domain_username>@<DOMAIN NAME> . -k Specifies to obtain the key for the ticket principal from a key table. So you are not prompted to enter the password for the ticket principal, and -t will refer to the keytab file specified
Example:
Test Data : mosmbad.dom, mouser (domain user) Command   : sudo kinit -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Note :
The domain name in above commands must be in UPPERCASE to avoid KDC mismatch issues while getting credentials.
Also check the ticket time with klist -a, (time of MoSMB server and AD should be always synced or Kerberos authentication may fail)
Generating Kerberos Ticket (Releases after March 2022)

In new releases, user will NOT be needed to acquire a ticket manually. /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries will be in the format of user@DOMAIN / cifs/user.domain@DOMAIN for standalone server, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive . To generate a ticket on the server, MoSMB will use principal name from domain_list.txt, match against the one present in mosmb.keytab and use it to generate the kerberos ticket.
Example for STANDALONE Server

# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM # klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
   KVNO Principal---- --------------------------------------------
    .
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
   2 mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM
Example for CLUSTER
# cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM

# klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
    KVNO Principal---- -------------------------------------------- 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM 
   3 cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
Verifying a Kerberos Ticket
To verify if the ticket is acquired for the domain_user, run the klist command.
Example:

Command : sudo klist -A Output  : Ticket cache: FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt Default principal: mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM Valid starting       Expires              Service principal 01/16/2020 11:33:50  01/16/2020 21:33:50  krbtgt/MOSMBAD.DOM@MOSMBAD.DOM renew until 01/17/2020 11:33:45
Note :
By default, the Kerberos ticket is valid for 10 hours .
The Kerberos ticket defaults may be modified by the Group Policy Management → Kerberos Policy → Security Settings in the AD.
Muti-Domain kerberos configuration
Carry out following changes in /etc/krb5.conf and /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf

Set default cache path to a directory location other than /tmp directory. DIR / KCM cache type is used in case of the multiple Kerberos tickets. default_ccache_name = DIR:<directory path> OR default_ccache_name = KCM: in /etc/krb5.conf
Example :
[libdefaults] #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid} default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/Kerberos_tickets default_realm = MOSMBAD1.DOM
Mention default realm as the domain to which server machine is joined in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
In the realms section mention all the realms in file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf
Example :
[realms] MOSMBAD1.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom admin_server = mo-ad1.mosmbad1.dom } MOSMBAD2.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom admin_server = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom }

Note: In case of common global name space across sites. 1. Add only local keytab on the server side. 2. For sssd configuration to work MoSMB servers must be part of all domains. 3. Generation of only local Kerberos ticket would be sufficient. (instead of DIR cache path in /etc/krb5.conf mention FILE path and file name)
LDAP Client Configurations
To enable the  ACL view on the client side you are required to perform the following operations, in case of Kerberos authentication:
A. Add a BASE entry in the LDAP configuration file /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) . The format should be as follows:
BASE   dc=example,dc=com
Example:
BASE    dc=mosmbad,dc=dom
B. Add the entry for the LDAP URI OR HOST to the /etc/openldap/ldap.conf (centOS) or /etc/ldap/ldap.conf (ubuntu/debian) file. The format should be as follows:

URI    ldap://<FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
   OR
   HOST    <FQDN_OF_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY_SERVER>
Example :
URI    ldap://mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom OR HOST    mosmb-winad.mosmbad.dom
Note :
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the LDAP related configuration, similar to above examples.
sssd Configuration

sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users.
After carrying out the domain join of the MoSMB server make sure that the sssd configurations are in place.
If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on the server machine.
Create a sssd configuration file /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf and it should contain parameters as shown in image below.
sssd configuration file location must be at /etc/sssd/sssd.conf with permissions of file set to 600 (-rw-------) and same with /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf
Start sssd service with service sssd start .
Note :
Restart the sssd service after making the sssd configurations as in the above image.
When MoSMB server is joined to Multiple domains, make sure all domain entries are present in the sssd related configuration, similar to above example.
Make entries of all domains MoSMB server is joined to as explained below.
domain_list.txt Configuration for sssd

For sssd related settings simply create a file named domain_list.txt at the /etc/mosmb/ location and mention the domain to which the MoSMB server is joined. Ensure that you make an entry for each domain on a new line.
Example:
#cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosmbad.dom

For sssd related settings simply create a file named /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of just domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt mosuer@MOSMBAD.DOM
In new releases, /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt will now use SPN entries instead of domain names. Entries should be in the format of user@DOMAIN, similar to the SPNs and will be case sensitive.
Example: #cat /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt cifs/my-cluster.mosmbad.dom@MOSMBAD.DOM
nsswitch.conf Configuration

Verify that nsswitch.conf file is configured to use SSSD as a provider
Open /etc/nsswitch.conf file and verify that it points to sss for the name service switch

passwd:     files sss
shadow:     files sss
group:      files sss
netgroup:   files sss

MoSMB Service Not Starting

If mosmb service fails to start, check the mosmb.log from the log folder (Default is /var/log) for Error messages. 
In case there is json syntax error you can use the mosmb json syntax validation script
Configuration Errors
The mosmb.log should indicate if any configuration errors pertaining to the json parameters are encountered. Accordingly use following to correct thoes.
Verify if the MoSMB share path mentioned in the configuration actually exists on the server.
Verify if the persistent store path mentioned in the MoSMB configuration actually exists on the server.

Verify if a valid combination of Authentication Modes and Authorization Modes is in use: i. When the authentication mode is krb5 , the permitted authorization modes are posix and ntacl . ii. When the authentication is ntlmLocal , the permitted authorization mode is posix .
Verify if the plugin path mentioned in the MoSMB configuration is correct.
Verify that the performance parameters like number of threads, buffer size and pre-processor threads are not exceeding the available resources. Refer to Performance Tuning
Verify if the bind interface value mentioned is actually an active interface on the server.
Refer to the MoSMB configuration parameters for further details.
Verify the required ports are open
Refer to Enable ports in firewall for MoSMB
License error in logs
For any Licensing related errors refer to section MoSMB Licensing Issues below
MoSMB Licensing Issues

To start the MoSMB server you require a trial license or a product key. Note : If the MoSMB server is a GCP instance then the following licensing scheme is not applicable. For more detailed information on Licensing refer to MoSMB License Manager page
Online Product Activation
Verify if the MoSMB server machine has a working internet connection.
Verify if the MoSMB server's network adapters are up.
Verify if the firewall policies allow port 443 to connect to the MoSMB license servers.
Verify if https://wyday.com/limelm/ is reachable from the MoSMB server.
Verify if the time and date settings of the MoSMB server are correct or up-to-date.
Verify if a valid product key is provided in the MoSMB configuration file for Licenses with product key.

Verify if the product key has reached the maximum number of activations limit. The information on the maximum limit for the number of machines the product key can be activated is given while providing the product key.
Online Trial Activation
Verify if the MoSMB server has a working internet connection.
Verify if MoSMB server's network adapters are up.
Verify if the firewall policies allow port 443 to connect to the MoSMB license servers.
Verify if https://wyday.com/limelm/ is reachable from the MoSMB server.
Verify if the time and date settings for the MoSMB server machine are correct or up-to-date.
Offline Product Activation
Verify if the MoSMB server's network adapters are up.
Verify if the product key mentioned in the MoSMB configuration and the product key used while activating the license is the same.
Make sure you have followed the steps for activation as per the offline product activation using product key
Offline Trial Activation
Verify if the trialresponse.xml file that is provided to you is placed at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location.
Verify if the trialrequest.xml is generated on the same MoSMB server machine on which you need to activate the MoSMB license.
Verify if the MoSMB server's network adapters are up.
Failed to check activation within grace period

When the Error - Failed to check product activation within grace period is seen in mosmb.log It would indicate that the product key was activated online on the server machine BUT now the server machine is not able to reach to | limelm site To activate the product key again, deactivate the current product key activation by running /etc/mosmb/license/.mosmb_license_deactivate Then follow offline activation steps
MoSMB Share Not Accessible
Check the mosmb.log for any obvious error messages that would indicate what is the problem.
Check if mosmb service is running, check pid or process information using ps

# service mosmb status
# pidof mosmb
# ps -aux| grep mosmb
Verify if port 445 is open for the TCP communication.
Verify if the TCP port mentioned in the MoSMB configuration file is also configured on the client machine and is open for communication over TCP.
If error is related to krb5, Refer to the Troubleshooting for the AD Kerberos MoSMB share access when authentication mode used for share access is krb5
If error is related to gss-api refer to the troubleshooting section for gss-api errors
If the authentication mode is ntlmLocal make sure that the (local linux) MoSMB user is created on the MoSMB server machine and you are accessing the share via the same user.

check the mosmb_audit.log for login from the user for whom the access is failing, to check if Login attempt is failing or user has successfully logged in.  Refer here for more details on the mosmb_audit.log
If Login is successful, Verify if the user has the permission to access the share for the authentication mode used (krb5, ntlm-AD or ntlm_local) as mentioned in Permission Issues section below.

If a previously working node is removed and Re Joined to AD , for ntlmAD to work after such a change, you will need to re run mosmb_passwd -m and restart mosmb service too. Also old kerberos ticket on client side may prevent you from accessing the share. You need to purge the cache to remove old cluster/node kerberos ticket with klist purge OR user can sign out and signing to the client. OR The kerberos cache will expire in scheduled 10 hours and next access will renew the ticket automatically.
MoSMB Share Not Accessible on Ubuntu 22.04 OR error seen with mosmb_passwd binary
possible error seen with mosmb_passwd :
ValueError: unsupported hash type md4
Verify if openssl configuration required for Ubuntu 22.04 are done
If the server machines have openssl version 3 and above configuration changes are required. Check openssl version with
openssl version
Open /etc/ssl/openssl.cnf
Existing/Default Configuration :

[provider_sect]
   default = default_sect
   [default_sect]
   # activate = 1
Edits/Required Configuration :

[provider_sect]
   default = default_sect legacy = legacy_sect [openssl_init]
   providers = provider_sect
 
   [default_sect] activate = 1 [legacy_sect] activate = 1
MoSMB Share Not Accessible for only a particular user

If access fails for a user, make sure audit logs are enabled and verify login attempts from the user for mosmb nodes. Check the mosmb_audit.log to see if Login attempt is failing or user has successfully logged in.

If login is successful check permission issues. 
If login is failed check the authentication password/domain information
If no login attempt message is seen check mosmb.log for MO_ERROR or MO_WARN logs
Permission Issues
Verify that the correct user permissions are set on the MoSMB server side and the same user is used to access the share and create or edit the shared files or directories.
POSIX Authorization Settings
NTACL Authorization Settings
Viewing File / Directory Permissions
Verify if port 88 is open on both the MoSMB server and the AD machine. This is required by the LDAP server.
When the authentication mode is krb5 verify if the Kerberos ticket is present on the MoSMB server.
In case the authentication mode is krb5 verify if all the LDAP client configuration settings are carried out.
In case the authentication mode is krb5 verify if all the SSSD related settings are carried out.
Verify if the package necessary for generating UUID is installed on the MoSMB server. If not please install the package.
ID Mapper Not Working Correctly
Verify if the sssd service is running on the MoSMB server.
check with id command that the AD user's details are retrieved and it does print the user ID and group IDs. In case it is failing to do so, try restarting the sssd service.
Verify the nsswitch.conf file and make sure that .sss is mentioned in the configuration.

Changes to the AD group membership are slow to take effect. The issue could be because of the sssd cache. When you change the primary group of the user then the old entry in the sssd cache becomes invalid. For new changes to take effect restart the sssd service and run the following command: sss_cache -E OR sss_cache -u <user-name>

If you are considering EVERYONE present in NTFS (Everyone includes the owner, group and other members) as Others (in Linux this means all the members other than the owner and group members) mapping will fail.
Note : In the case of user and group mapping, MoSMB currently supports only the primary group mapping.
Issue with cluster keytab. Cluster fqdn access not working. (gss-api errors in mosmb.log)
Check time is in sync with the AD
Make sure the keytab creation is correctly done using ktpass on AD by the Admin (w.o. any errors or warning during the creation) using correct CRYPTO values used to generate keys in the keytab.

Use following PS cmdlet to check the spn is correctly mapped the special user setspn -Q cifs/<cifs-server-name> And use the follwoing PS cmdlet to check the user's SPN Get-ADUser <username> -property ServicePrincipalName
The DNS forward lookup works for the cluster fqdn and reverse lookup work correctly for cluster node ips
Check firewall for correct ports opened for kerberos
Check that the special user created for keytab creation in the ktpass 'mapuser', has following properties set:

Go to Active Directory users and computers 
User properties -> user account -> password never expires
User properties -> user account -> Correct encryption support (in accordance with the CRYPTO values used in ktpass) is enabled, e.g. This account supports Kerberos AES 256 bit encryption This account supports Kerberos AES 128 bit encryption You can also check the encryptions used in the keytab with -e option of klist klist -kte /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
OR use following PS cmdlet to check the user's supported encryption types Get-ADUser <username> -property KerberosEncryptionType
Check KVno from keytab against the special user.

Note the msDS-KeyVersionNumber from the o/p of following PS cmdlet and compare with kvno in the keytab from the output of klist ran on the mosmb.keytab Get-ADUser <username> -property msDS-KeyVersionNumber klist -kt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
In mosmb.json, check that globalSection is set to have enableHighAvailability to yes
Check the o/p of klist -A to check a valid krb5 tkt is fetched during mosmb startup ( mosmb will use the mosmb.keytab and domain_list.txt to do a kinit )
Check sssd.conf, krb5.conf, ldap.conf are set in accordance with the mosmb documentation
Check permitted encryption types from the krb5 crypto-policies
cat /etc/krb5.conf.d/crypto-policies

If setup was working fine previously, and now we are getting "Preauthentication failed" error in mosmb.log, verify that the password for special user used for cluster keytab creation is not expired or changed. If so, need to recreate the mosmb.keytab using ktpass

Refer to the AD Kerberos Troubleshooting Guide .
Troubleshooting Guide on RDMA (SMBDirect) communication on the MoSMB server
Refer → Basic RDMA(SMBDirect) Setup Guide .
ZooKeeper Troubleshooting
1. Error 1:
Unable to start AdminServer, exiting abnormally
Solution : Check for admin.serverPort configuration in zoo.cfg :
#admin server port configuration admin.serverPort=8082
Make sure port 8082 is free for use, if not free, use some other port
netstat -ntlp | grep 8082
2. Error 2:
java.net.BindException: Address already in use (Bind failed)
Solution : Check if ports configured are available, if not free then use some other port combination
netstat -ntlp | grep 4888 & netstat -ntlp | grep 5888 on all ZooKeeper Server machines.

Example :	
server.1 = <ip_server1>:4888:5888
server.2 = <ip_server2>:4888:5888
server.3 = <ip_server3>:4888:5888
Here 4888 used for follower nodes to connect to the leader node and 5888 used for leader election.
3. MoSMB service not starting due to older zookeeper versions refer :- Uninstalling previous zookeeper version
4. Make sure after successful installation all required zookeeper libraries are present on the zookeeper server.
Microsoft Management Console (MMC)
Manage File Shares with Shared Folders MMC Snap-in. Close sessions and close open file handles can be managed via MMC
Open mmc on a windows → Navigate to the Start Menu and select Run, type 'mmc'.
On the MMC console from File menu select Add or Remove snap-ins
Select and add Shared Folders snap-in
In Shared Folder properties → Enter the node fqdn or ip and connect.
Check Shares to list the smb-shares on the nodes.
Check Sessions to list the smb-sessions connected to the node.
Check Open Files to list all the open files on the node.
MAC slow browsing performance
NOTE: sonoma 14.6.1 onwards nsmb.conf does not help, refer to below points for older MAC OS versions
To improve MAC smb client's browsing performance, following settings can be tried 1. edit /etc/nsmb.conf

[default]
dir_cache_off=yes
aapl_off=true
signing_required=no
file_ids_off=yes
dir_ids_off=yes
defaults write com.apple.desktopservices DSDontWriteNetworkStores -bool TRUE
On terminal run defaults write com.apple.desktopservices DSDontWriteNetworkStores -bool TRUE
check the setting with defaults read com.apple.desktopservices DSDontWriteNetworkStores
Restart your MAC once these changes are done
2. Disable icon previews and size calculation

connect to share
Browse to share
Press CMD + j (or rt click-> show view options )
  Disable “Calculate all sizes”
  Disable “Show icon preview”
Select “Use as Defaults”
3. some more view options
Going further u can disable showing 'date modified' and 'size' in the view options
4. Turn off indexing spotlight indexing can be turned off with
sudo mdutil -a -i off /Volumes/<share-name>
To see whether Spotlight indexing is enabled for a volume:

mdutil -s -v /Volumes/share-name
 example: mdutil -s /Volumes/share1 /System/Volumes/Data/Volumes/share1:
    Indexing and searching disabled.
5. 311 smb protocol in mosmb.json on releases 072 and later Try changing the max smb protocol to 311 on the mosmb server in mosmb.json file and restart mosmb service
maxSmbProtocol = 311
6. If access is still slow; try some more network settings
try following n/w settings on MAC , run from terminal

sysctl -w kern.ipc.maxsockbuf=8388608
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.sendspace=2097152
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.recvspace=2097152
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.delayed_ack=2
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.win_scale_factor=8
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.autorcvbufmax=33554432
sysctl -w net.inet.tcp.autosndbufmax=33554432
No restart required, if restarted the settings will be lost. Some more settings

sysctl -w net.smb.fs.kern_deadtimer=0
sysctl -w net.smb.fs.kern_hard_deadtimer=0
sysctl -w net.smb.fs.kern_soft_deadtimer=0

In addition, if your Mac needs above network settings to be retained on reboot.
Create a file named /etc/sysctl.conf in your local /etc folder
This needs again to be done as root!

Go to the command line, become root and fire up your preferred editor (vi) by typing vi /etc/sysctl.conf
Put the following into the newly created file,

vi /etc/sysctl.conf 
kern.ipc.maxsockbuf=8388608
net.inet.tcp.sendspace=2097152
net.inet.tcp.recvspace=2097152
net.inet.tcp.delayed_ack=2
net.inet.tcp.win_scale_factor=8
net.inet.tcp.autorcvbufmax=33554432
net.inet.tcp.autosndbufmax=33554433
net.smb.fs.kern_deadtimer=0
net.smb.fs.kern_hard_deadtimer=0
net.smb.fs.kern_soft_deadtimer=0

and save the file. The file rights must be set to:
owner root:wheel and -rw-r--r--
please make sure by typing ls -al /etc/sysctl.conf on the command line and if not, change it by running
chown root:wheel /etc/sysctl.conf
and
chmod 0644 /etc/sysctl.conf from the command line
