cmdpipe
cmdpipe is a helper script located in /etc/mosmb/scripts directory and it support following tasks -
Reload the MoSMB configuration file - /etc/mosmb/mosm.json after adding new share
Update MoSMB logging at runtime
Notifies SMB Command frequency count
Display the MoSMB server statistics
Prerequisites
Python and pip of same version
Command-line JSON processor : jq package
MoSMB service should be running
Usage

Command: cd /etc/mosmb/scripts/ Command: ./cmdpipe -h Usage: ./cmdpipe <command>
       command:
       config_reload_add : Reloads mosmb config to add new share
       add_runtime_logging : Notifies mosmb of the changes in /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf
       smb_frequency_count : Notifies smb command frequency count in  <log folder from mosmb.json>/mosmb_smb_frequency_cnt.log file
       set_maintenance_mode : Set the MoSMB server in Maintenance Mode. Note: Available from Release 069 get_mosmb_config : Get the configuration parameters of MoSMB server. Note: Available from Release 087 statson : Starts collecting statistics
       domainlist_reload : Reloads the domainlist.txt contents in mosmb Note: Available from Release 100

Use the following options for mosmb config reload
       -n : Specifies share name
       -t : Specifies share type
       -h : Display this help message and exit
       Share type can be one if these two : VFS or S3
       Following is an Example:
       ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n share_name -t vfs
       OR
       ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n share_name -t S3
           
       Use the following options for dumping stats into csv periodically
       -p : Specifies csv path. Default is /var/log/
       -i : Specifies time period in seconds. This is the repeat interval for the dump job. Default is 300
       -c : Display stats on 'console'
       -h : Shows statson help message and Exit
       -n : Number count for stats operation
       Following is an Example:
       ./cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 600
       ./cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 600 -c : Display the stats on console after every '-i' interval
       ./cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 600 -c -n 5: Display the stats on console after every '-i' interval for '-n' number of time
Examples
config_reload_add
Prerequisites:
MoSMB should be running.
New share should be added to MoSMB configurations /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json either manually or by mosmbcli set --json command for share option
Usage:

Command: /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe config_reload_add -h Use the following options for mosmb config reload
       -n : Specifies share name
       -t : Specifies share type
       -h : Display this help message and exit
       Share type can be one if these two : VFS or S3
       Following is an Example:
       ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n share_name -t vfs
       OR
       ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n share_name -t S3
Example:

Command: ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n "Local xfs" -t vfs Success
       Command executed successfully
add_runtime_logging
Prerequisites:
MoSMB should be running.
Logging level changes will take effect for mosmb logs as per changes in /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf
Usage:

Command: /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe add_runtime_logging -h 1) First make the required log level changes in  /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf
       2) Now run the command /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe add_runtime_logging
Example:

Command: ./cmdpipe add_runtime_logging Logging Reload Command initialized
       Success
       Command executed successfully
smb_frequency_count
Prerequisites:
MoSMB should be running. Note: File mosmb_smb_frequency_cnt.log will be created at the MoSMB logs directory mentioned in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json.
Usage:
Command: ./cmdpipe smb_frequency_count -h Simply run the command /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe smb_frequency_count
Example:

Command: ./cmdpipe smb_frequency_count Getting frequency count of smb commands
       Success
       Command executed successfully
set_maintenance_mode
Prerequisites:
MoSMB should be running to enable or disable maintenance mode. Note: New option available only from MoSMB release 069 onwards.
Usage:

Command: /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe set_maintenance_mode -h Usage options for mosmb set maintenance mode -o : Specifies option to enable or disable the maintenance mode
       -h : Display this help message and exit
Example:

Command: ./cmdpipe set_maintenance_mode -o enable Success
       Command executed successfully
       Also mosmb.log will show the notification message as follows - [MO_CRITICAL]:[SMBCORE]:[TID 42496]: MoSMB server has entered into Maintenance Mode. No new connections/sessions will be accepted now.
get_mosmb_config
Prerequisites:
MoSMB should be running to get the current static and dynamic configurations. Note: New option available only from MoSMB release 087 onwards.
Usage:

Command: ./cmdpipe get_mosmb_config -p <path> -f humanreadable OR ./cmdpipe get_mosmb_config -p <path> -f csv Usage options for mosmb get configuration parameters -p : Specify path where the configuration parameters will be dumped. Default is path pointed out by logsPath parameter in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json
       -f : Specify format in which the configuration parameters will be dumped. Default is 'humanreadable'
       -h : Display this help message and exit
Example:

Command: ./cmdpipe get_mosmb_config -f csv Sending get server config params command to the MoSMB server.
     Command executed successfully
     Dump of the configuration parameters is at /var/log/ -- csv file named mosmb_config_params_dump.csv is created.
statson
Prerequisites:

MoSMB should be running. Note: Following files will be created when -p option is given depending on the build type 1. preprocessor_stats.csv 2. server_stats.csv 3. worker_thread_stats.csv 4. smbdirect_stats.csv 5. processed_stats.csv
Usage:

Command: ./cmdpipe statson -h Usage: cmdpipe statson [OPTION]...
       MOSMB server statistics
       Optional Arguments to statson
               -p, --path       Specifies csv path. Default is /var/log/
               -i, --interval   Specifies time period in seconds. This is the repeat interval for the dump job. Default is 300
               -c, --console    Display stats on 'console'
               -n, --number     Number count for stats operation
               -h, --help       Shows this help message and Exit
       
       Following Abbreviations used while showing output on console using '-c' option
       
       RIO(Read IOPS), WIO(Write IOPS), TIOPs(Total IOPS), RT(Read Throughput), WT(Write Throughput),
       TT(MB/s)(Total Throughput MB/s), RL(Read Latency), WL(Write Latency), SRL(Storage Read Latency),
       SWL(Storage Write Latency), FO(Total File/Pipe Opened), ES(Total Established Sessions),
       DC(Total Disconnects), PV(Password Violations), PE(Total Permission Error)
Example: Following is an Example:

Support Added for .prom file format from version 093 onwards MoSMB version 093 onwards cmdpipe supports .prom file format to collect metric in the Prometheus text exposition format. (key value pair) More details on how to use Prometheus to monitor mosmb metrics is available HERE

Use the following options for dumping stats into csv/prom periodically
  -p : Specifies stats path. Default path for 'csv' format is /var/log/. Note: While using 'prom' stats format, it is mandatory to use same path for both i.e. 'cmdpipe' and 'node_exporter' ('--collector.textfile.directory' option) of prometheus. -f : Specifies format in which the stats will be dumped (prom / csv) . Default is 'csv' format
  -i : Specifies time period in seconds. This is the repeat interval for the dump job. Default is 300
  -c : Displays stats on 'console'
  -h : Shows statson help message and Exit
  -n : Number count for stats operation
   
Following is an Example: ./cmdpipe statson -p /var/lib/node_exporter -f prom -i 600 -c -n 5 i.e. Display the stats on console after every '-i' interval for '-n' number of time, save stats in processed_stats.prom file in folder /var/lib/node_exporter

domainlist_reload
Prerequisites:

MoSMB should be running to reload the domain's list from domain_list.txt And it will also check if the domain has a whitelist present, if yes, it will use the IPs mentioned in the whitelist of the specific domain to reconnect to AD Note: New option available only from MoSMB release 100 onwards.
Usage:
Command: ./cmdpipe domainlist_reload
Error Codes
sssd configuration
sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users.
If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on all the MoSMB server machine in cluster.
Create a sssd configuration file for MoSMB /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf and it should contain parameters as shown below.

[sssd] domains = mosmbad.dom config_file_version = 2
   services = nss, pam [domain/mosmbad.dom] ad_domain = mosmbad.dom
   krb5_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM krb5_keytab = /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab realmd_tags = manages-system joined-with-adcli
   cache_credentials = True
   id_provider = ad
   krb5_store_password_if_offline = True
   default_shell = /bin/bash ldap_id_mapping = True use_fully_qualified_names = True
   fallback_homedir = /home/%u@%d
   access_provider = ad
sssd configuration files must be at /etc/sssd/ with permissions of file set to 600 (-rw-------) and for both /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf
Start sssd service with service sssd start .
nsswitch.conf Configuration
Verify that nsswitch.conf file is configured to use SSSD as a provider
Open /etc/nsswitch.conf file and verify that it points to sss for the name service switch

passwd:     files sss
   shadow:     files sss
   group:      files sss netgroup:   files sss
Note :
Restart the sssd service after making any changes in the sssd configurations.
Two authentication modes of the MoSMB server provide the POSIX ACL view:
Kerberos Authentication
ntlmLocal Authentication
POSIX ACL is Portable Operating System Interface - Access Control Lists.
Note : POSIX ACL for MoSMB means only the Server user's ID mapping to the MoSMB share and its included files and directories.

MoSMB Configuration
For the POSIX ACL view  MoSMB share configurations are required as well. Refer to Share Configuration .
Kerberos
In case of krb5 Authentication you must:
Execute a chown command.
In this chown comand you must set permissions for the share path to a domain user and domain group .
chown -R “<domain-user>@<domain-name>”: “<group-name>@<domain-name>” <share path>
Example:
chown  -R "mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM":"domain users@MOSMBAD.DOM" /opt/share/
chown -R “<domain-name>\<domain-user>”: “<domain-name>\<group-name>” <share path>
Example:
chown  -R "MOSMBAD\mouser":"MOSMBAD\domain users" /opt/share/

The ACL view will not display proper names in the Security tabs if: 1. The ldap client configuration is not properly carried out. 2. The File or Directory owner/group is not a domain user. In such cases the names will be displayed as:
"LOCAL ACCOUNT" or "S-1-5-113" which is mapped to the owner permissions.
"SYSTEM" which is mapped to group permissions.
"Everyone" which is mapped to other permissions.
If properly configured, the domain user name will be displayed as shown underneath:
NTLM
In case of posix ACL and ntlm_local authentication you are required to:
Set the permissions of the share path to the Linux local user.
Add the User group as a MoSMB user using the mosmb_passwd binary.
Refer to ntlm_local Authentication for details. The ownership of the share path should be set to the Linux user and the user group must be set as the MoSMB user.
sudo chown -R <linux-user-name> : <linux-group-name> <share path>
Example :
Test Data : motest, motest, /opt/share Command   : sudo chown -R motest:motest /opt/share
The user name and group will be displayed on the client side as follows:
In order to enable Advance Posix ACL support for a MoSMB share which is mentioned in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json configurations, follow bellow steps:
MoSMB configurations : /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json
In shareSection under aclStrategy set parameter acl to advancePosix

1 Permission mapping Windows to Linux
2 Setting Permissions On Linux
3 Inheritance
4 Rules and Limitations
Permission mapping Windows to Linux

In the Windows world, permissions are more granular than Linux. Each file system object is associated with multiple users/groups. Each of which may contain around fourteen/fifteen permissions. When file object is accessed by both the world Linux and Windows then to maintain compatibility and consistency, mapping needs to be done. The mapping is challenging as in Linux only three permissions are defined vs fifteen in windows as seen below.
Setting Permissions On Linux
Extended permissions are set via setfacl command on linux side.

Example: Setting Extended ACEs - setfacl -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder> Setting Extended ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs - setfacl -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder>
Check extended/default permissions with getfacl command on linux side.
Example: getfacl <absolute path of file/folder>
Linux filesystem allows only Owner and superusers (sudo) to set or modify permissions of files and folders.
In Linux world “owner“, “primary group” and “other” can not be removed, though all permissions can be removed for the same except for the owner. Read permissions for the owner can not be removed.
Example: file/folder can have Owner:Group:Other permissions to any combination of rwx but lowest it can be set to as r--:---:---
If the parent has execute permissions for users children of that parent will be enumerated; otherwise they won't be.
Inheritance
All files and folders in the share have Inheritance enabled by default.
All inherited permissions are by default applied as This folder subfolder and files .
Permissions will be inherited on basis of applies to section mentioned while setting new permissions or inheriting existing as follows,
Rules and Limitations
On any share inheritance is always enabled. Disable inheritance button on windows client (Properties → security → Advance → Disable Inheritance) is not supported.
Only Allow ACE type is supported. Deny type from windows property tab is not supported.
Windows system ACL permission requests will not be supported.
Only Owner and super user is allowed to add new permissions, modify newly added permissions or remove newly added permissions on shares, files and folders.
Recommended to add permissions in default ACEs from server side on shared path for inheritance to be enabled.

When existing ACEs permissions on share root are modified; it will remove the inherited(default) permissions of that specific shared path. The user will be warned about this. This behavior is in adherence to Windows smb share.

Behavior of  checkbox - Only apply these permissions to objects and/or containers within this container - When any new user/group permission is added with this checkbox selected then it will be applied as "This folder only" for the current folder and to it's existing immediate children as "This folder only"

Special case - EVERYONE SID We give inherited permissions higher priority over Everyone when Everyone has lower permissions than inherited permissions then only inherited permissions will be considered.

It is not recommended to mix linux local users or root with AD users/groups in permissions - Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display. When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux.
Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display.

When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux.
Access time mask or acl mask is to be kept as rwx always (mask::rwx & default:mask::rwx), as permissions seen on windows client side are not filtered.

Creation time mask or mosmb mask (fileCreationMask & directoryCreationMask) can be kept as per the need. Please not that In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx
In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx
In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx
Advance Posix ACL module supports multiple group mapping for any user up to 256 groups.

3 How to run the playbook
MoSMB Ansible based installer
Important terminologies
The node on which ansible is installed is called ' ansible control node' .
The nodes on which ansible will perform installation and configuration are called as ' ansible client nodes' (eg. mosmb,zookeeper installation).
Playbook is the main script which triggers the installation.
hosts file is called as ansible inventory.
A unit of task is called as ansible role .

It is advisable to keep the ansible control node different from ansible client nodes, but you can use one of the ansible client node as ansible control node. This installer can be used to install MoSMB on a fleet of VM's which can be located either on prem or on the cloud. The installer is split into 3 playbooks and 6 roles.
Playbook
The playbook is responsible for driving all the tasks/roles. There are 3 different playbooks that can be run.
mosmb_ecosystem.yml
mosmb_storage.yml
mosmb_install.yml
Respective config files and uninstall playbooks are:
mosmb_ecosystem_config.yml & mosmb_ecosystem_uninstall.yml
mosmb_storage_config.yml
momsb_install_config.yml & mosmb_uninstall.yml
Roles
Roles are nothing but individual task entities. Currently there are 6 roles inside the roles folder.
dc-config
domain-join
maprfs
mosmb
zookeeper
preinstall

The final preinstall role is consumed by mapr installer to trigger mosmb installation either before or after
mapr installation. If we want to install mosmb after mapr installation then this directory needs to be renamed
to postinstall.
Tasks Completed by each playbook
Mosmb Ecosystem Playbook
Configure DNS entries for individual nodes as well as for a common cluster name.
Create a service account and generate keytab against common cluster name with this account.
Edit resolv.conf so that the node can locate nameservers and active directory realm's.
Join nodes to multiple domains.
Edit sssd configuration against multiple domains.
Add cluster keytab entries to existing keytab.
Generate kerberos ticket.
Mosmb Storage Playbook

Currently maprfs is the only storage that can be configured.
For mapr role, if the node to be setup is an outside mapr cluster node then we perform some extra steps like
adding mapr repo, installing mapr client to be able to generate mapr service ticket. This steps are skipped if
the node is itself one of the mapr node. Following are the common steps for both scenarios.
Install posix client
Configure fuse.conf
Generate mapr cluster ticket
Mount maprfs
Mosmb Install Playbook
Download the specified version of zookeeper and configure it to run as a service.
Configure and install MoSMB
Prerequisites
Ansible can be downloaded and installed from this link. https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/installation_guide/intro_installation.html

For ansible to work with linux clients you need to generate ssh public and private key using ssh-keygen
command.
This public private key pair should be generated against user on the remote machine. Ansible uses this user
for it remote machine automation. Root user can be used otherwise the user needs to have sudo privileges.
Ex. If user1 is a user on node1 and ansible needs to communicate with node1 for configuration then
generate an ssh public private key as shown below. - Generating public/private rsa key pair .
ssh-keygen -t rsa -C "user1"
Enter file in which to save the key (/root/.ssh/id_rsa): /home/user1/.ssh/user1 Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase): Enter same passphrase again:

The above code will generate user1 and user1.pub . - Copy this user1.pub to the client machine which needs to
be controlled using ansible. You can do that using the below command.
ssh-copy-id -f -i /home/user1/.ssh/user1.pub user1@node1

A domain admin user is also required by ansible to communicate with the domain controller.
Ansible uses WinRM for communicating with remote windows clients. Therefore you need to run the following
two scripts for setting WinRM up on you remote windows machine (for us it is the Domain Controller).

According to ansible documentation, there is bug in WinRM service that limits the amount of memory
available to run WinRM scripts. This is applicable only for powershell 3.0 version This powershell script will fix that problem.

$url = " https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jborean93/ansible-windows/master/scripts/Install-WMF3Hotfix.ps1 "
$file = "$env:temp\Install-WMF3Hotfix.ps1"
(New-Object -TypeName System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile($url, $file)
powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -File $file -Verbose
NOTE : If you face issues/errors in above step, one can try raw copy of the script file and run that.
This powershell script sets up the WinRM service so that ansible can communicate with it over HTTPS.

$url = " https://github.com/ansible/ansible-documentation/blob/devel/examples/scripts/ConfigureRemotingForAnsible.ps1 "
$file = "$env:temp\ConfigureRemotingForAnsible.ps1"
(New-Object -TypeName System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile($url, $file)
powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -File $file

If fetching the above urls fails, then please notify it to the IT administrator. And if there are errors running the script, see if copying the script content as raw helps Now you can check if the WinRM listener service is running by using the following command.
winrm enumerate winrm/config/Listener

Currently ansible requires certain modules which are not a part of its base installation. Multiple modules
together form a collection. You can install all the collections required by mosmb ansible installer by running
the following command in the ansible/collections directory.
ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml
Ansible controller can communicate with windows client using 5 different mechanisms.
Basic
Kerberos
NTLM
Certificate
CredSSP

Out of this we will be using Kerberos based authentication. For this we need credentials of a user present in
the domain admins group. Also make sure that these packages are installed on your controller node. For CentOS
yum -y install gcc python3-devel krb5-devel krb5-libs krb5-workstation
For Ubuntu
sudo apt-get install python3-dev libkrb5-dev krb5-user
From python pip you need to install the following package
pip3 install pexpect pywinrm[kerberos]
Inventory
Ansible configuration
Configuration per playbook
This is the hosts file which contains information of the all the nodes. Multiple nodes can be grouped.

Nodes can be written as either ip addresses or hostnames. For information on ansible inventory
configuration please refer to this official ansible document. https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/user_guide/intro_inventory.html
Example structure of ansible inventory is given below.

mosmb_cluster is a group of nodes on which mosmb will be installed. ansible_connection=local can be added if a specific node is the control node. In such a case ssh public key need not be added to this node.

[mosmb_cluster]
10.1.1.10 ansible_connection=local
10.1.1.11
sbvm10
zookeeper_cluster is a group of nodes on which on zookeeper will be installed
domain-controller is a group of nodes on which mosmb's domain controller side configuration will be done. If kerberos based authentication is used then provide fqdn of the domain controller node

[dc] 
sbvm28.mosmbtestad.dom

These variables are used for establishing connection with windows domain controller nodes. Ansible uses WinRM for communicating with windows nodes. You can use ssh as well, but that needs to be set up

[dc:vars]
ansible_connection=winrm

Ansible user is domain admin user who has the permission to edit dns configuration and generate keytab
Its format is username@REALM

ansible_user=mouser@MOSMBTESTAD.DOM
ansible_passsword=Password
ansible_winrm_transport=kerberos
ansible_winrm_server_cert_validation=ignore ---

This is the ansible.cfg file which contains the configuration required by ansible to connect to the nodes. Example configuration: Enable the below inventory section if working google compute vm's (GCP cloud environment)

[inventory]
enable_plugins = gcp_compute
[defaults]
Set the location to ansible logs
log_path = /var/log/ansible.log
The remote user which will be used by ansible establish connection with its clients.
remote_user = user1
If using google compute vm's then below value should be commented
inventory = infra.gcp.yml
Location to the private key file which will be used by ansible to establish connection with its clients
private_key_file = ~/.ssh/user1

The installer is split into 3 different playbooks. 1. Ecosystem playbook This will trigger two of the above mentioned roles which are dc-config and
domain-join. So it will be doing domain controller side configuration and joining mosmb nodes to the
domain if required. To run the ecosystem installation you need to edit the mosmb_ecosystem_config.yml
file and run the mosmb_ecosystem.yml file. 2. Storage playbook This playbook was meant to mount different storages to mosmb nodes. Currently it
supports mounting maprfs only via the maprfs role. To run the storage installation you need to edit the
mosmb_storage_config.yml and run the mosmb_storage.yml file. 3. Install playbook This will trigger two of the above mentioned roles which are zookeeper and mosmb.
To run the MoSMB installation you need to edit the mosmb_install_config.yml and run mosmb_install.yml file.
Explanation regarding each variable is provided the same configuration file.
The mosmb package should be placed in ' ansible/roles/mosmb/packages' folder. Create this folder if it does not exists.
How to run the playbook

The playbook can be run using ansible-playbook command. The inventory file is passed as an argument to
this. If your remote user is root then the below command can be used for starting the installation.
ansible-playbook -i hosts <one of the above mentioned playbook> ansible-playbook -i hosts mosmb_install.yml

If your remote user is local user with sudo privileges then the below command example can be used for
starting the installation.

ansible-playbook -i hosts <one of the above mentioend playbook> --extra-vars "ansible_sudo_pass=sudopassword" ansible-playbook -i hosts mosmb_ecosystem.yml --extra-vars "ansible_sudo_pass=sudopassword"
The MoSMB Server is a client of the ZooKeeper Server.
To install the ZooKeeper Server, perform the following procedure:

Prerequisites
The ZooKeeper installation has the following dependencies:
Ubuntu / Debian
1. openjdk-8-jdk-headless
Redhat based Linux distributions
1. java-11-openjdk or higher
Zookeeper installation on Redhat based Linux distributions using rpm for New Releases after March 2022

For Cluster builds, a zookeeper rpm will also be provided along with MoSMB rpm. MoSMB-zookeeper rpm is present at : /opt/mosmb/package/mosmb_install To install you can use following command:
rpm -ivh /opt/mosmb/package/mosmb_install/mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1.x86_64.rpm
Verifying...                          ################################# [100%]
Preparing...                          ################################# [100%]
Updating / installing...
   1:mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1          ################################# [100%]
zookeeper is installed at : /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/
zookeeper will be installed at /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/ and user will need to make appropriate changes to zoo.cfg and then start zkServer as mentioned in above steps.
# /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkServer.sh start
Get ZooKeeper for other builds and distributions
1. Get zookeeper installer apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin
wget https://archive.apache.org/dist/zookeeper/zookeeper-3.6.3/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin.tar.gz
2. Extract downloaded packages
tar -xvzf apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin.tar.gz
Configure the ZooKeeper Server
1. Go to other extracted directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin 2. Copy sample ZooKeeper configuration file from /etc/mosmb/.
cp /etc/mosmb/mosmb_zoo.cfg conf/zoo.cfg
3.  Create ZooKeeper server data directory can be any preferred location other than /tmp.
mkdir -p /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb ..........
4.  ZooKeeper Standalone Server Configuration
5. ZooKeeper Cluster Server Configuration
For detailed information on the configuration parameters refer to the ZooKeeper Server Configuration Parameters page.
6. Create the myid file on the ZooKeeper server/s at the /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/ path.
Example on a Cluster ZooKeeper:

Test data                      : ZooKeeper Server IP list - 10.10.0.1, 10.10.0.2, 10.10.0.3 File on server 1 (10.10.0.1)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 1 (10.10.0.1) : 1 File on server 2 (10.10.0.2)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 2 (10.10.0.2) : 2 File on server 3 (10.10.0.3)   : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 3 (10.10.0.3) : 3
Setup zookeepr server side log rotation

This step is not mandatory. In case zookeeper restarts, the server side log located in the log folder of zookeeper installation path, (/opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/) is overwritten and old logs are over-written/lost. To avoid loss of old logs, one can do the following
1. A solution is to modify zkEnv.sh and set ZOO_LOG4J_PROP

$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP
if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ]
 ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE"
2. here add ROLLINGFILE

$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP
if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ]
  ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE, ROLLINGFILE "
3. Now every restart of mosmb-zookeeper service will have two log files, one current (.out) and one backed up with old logs (.log).
Start the ZooKeeper Server
1. Go to the ZooKeeper directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin and run the following command:
./bin/zkServer.sh start
This will create a ZooKeeper log file at location ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/ that will contain the ZooKeeper server related logs. 2. Check status.
Example for a Standalone ZooKeeper Server:
Command   : ./bin/zkServer.sh status Output    : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg Mode: Standalone
Example for a Cluster ZooKeeper Server:

Test data : ZooKeeper Server IP list - 10.10.0.1, 10.10.0.2, 10.10.0.3 Command   : ./bin/zkServer.sh status Output    : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg' Mode: Leader (This output will be different on all server one will be the leader and other servers will be Follower )

rpm -e can be used to uninstall the zookeeper rpm.
# rpm -e mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1.x86_64
Uninstall Previous older ZooKeeper Versions
1. Stop older version of ZooKeeper service (Older than zookeeper 3.6.3)
./bin/zkServer.sh stop
2. Go to zookeeper’s previous version's source directory
For zookeeper 3.4.5 - zookeeper3.4.5/src/c
For zookeeper 3.6.1 - apache-zookeeper-3.6.1/zookeeper-client/zookeeper-client-c/
3. Run command -
make clean make uninstall
4. Make sure all previous library files are not on the zookeeper server machine -
find /usr/ -name libzookeeper*
5. Continue with required ZooKeeper version build and installation.
ZooKeeper - MoSMB Configuration Settings
Note:
ZooKeeper is a centralized service for maintaining configuration information, naming, providing distributed synchronization, and providing group services.
You must make ZooKeeper settings to use the MoSMB server. This depends on the MoSMB edition, it will be required for MoSMB-CL edition & MoSMB-SA-S3 edition .

Standalone ZooKeeper Server : MoSMB is set by default to work with the local Standalone ZooKeeper Server , as shown in the Standalone ZooKeeper Server Settings image underneath (setting in mosmb.json).
Remote ZooKeeper Server : For Remote ZooKeeper Server Settings in mosmb.json you can state the Hostport parameter as:
<server_hostname> : <zookeeper_client_port> OR <server_ip_address> : <zookeeper_client_port>
Clustered ZooKeeper Server : If required you can make the Clustered ZooKeeper Server settings. Refer to the Clustered ZooKeeper Server Settings image underneath.
The detailed ZooKeeper Server Configuration parameters are provided in the Zookeeper Server Configuration File Details .
mosmb.json representation -  Standalone Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation -  Standalone Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation -  Standalone Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation -  Standalone Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation - Clustered Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation - Clustered Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation - Clustered Zookeeper Server Settings
mosmb.json representation - Clustered Zookeeper Server Settings
Note : A MoSMB server restart is mandatory in case any changes are made to the configuration file.

Server maintenance includes allowing user to perform clean start, stop operations along with dynamically adding a share and compute performance parameters depending on the MoSMB Server Machine Configurations.
Note :

* The MoSMB Server should be started in only one way at a time, that is either as binary or as a service.
   * The MoSMB Server will start when the product key or trial license is valid, persistent storage and share paths mentioned are available on the server. Refer to the MoSMB License Manager .

Service
User can perform a Start, Stop or Restart operations on the MoSMB service.
For any change/s in the MoSMB server configuration files /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json and/or /etc/mosmb/s3.json in any section/s it is mandatory to restart the MoSMB server.
Notes: : 1. systemctl support is available after release builds 073. 2. One can use Either service commands or systemctl commands throughout the cycle.
MoSMB Start
Command : EITHER service mosmb start OR systemctl start mosmb Output  : Starting MoSMB service...

When MoSMB is being used with ZooKeeper server, the sequence of starting these services is important as MoSMB is ZooKeeper's client and ZooKeeper takes the locks on certain files which is released after 200 secs timeout if the MoSMB / ZooKeeper server is exited abruptly or MoSMB / ZooKeeper server machine is rebooted.

1. Start ZooKeeper - Command : sh  /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkServer.sh start Output  : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg' Mode: Standalone Mode: Leader / Follower / Standalone this output will be different on individual servers depending on the MoSMB-ZooKeeper configurations 2. ZooKeeper Cleanup -
       ZooKeeper Cleanup is must when MoSMB or Zookeeper goes down (services or machines) while any smb operations are in progress.
       To Cleanup ZooKeeper nodes refer - MoSMB-ZooKeeper server cleanup 3. Start MoSMB - Command : EITHER service mosmb start OR systemctl start mosmb Output  : Starting MoSMB service...

When MoSMB is being used with ZooKeeper server, the sequence of starting these services is important as MoSMB is ZooKeeper client and ZooKeeper takes the locks on certain files which is released after 200 secs timeout if the MoSMB / ZooKeeper server is exited abruptly or MoSMB / ZooKeeper server machine is rebooted.

1. Start ZooKeeper on all nodes - Command : sh  /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkServer.sh start Output  : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg' Mode: Leader Mode: Leader / Follower this output will be different on individual servers depending on the MoSMB-ZooKeeper configurations 2. ZooKeeper Cleanup on all nodes -
       ZooKeeper Cleanup is must when MoSMB or Zookeeper goes down (services or machines) while any smb operations are in progress.
       To Cleanup ZooKeeper nodes refer - MoSMB-ZooKeeper server cleanup 3. Start MoSMB on all nodes - Command : EITHER service mosmb start OR systemctl start mosmb Output  : Starting MoSMB service...
Note :
* If MoSMB server and ZooKeeper server are configured on 2 separate machines and only MoSMB service/server goes down then ZooKeeper cleanup is not a requirement.
MoSMB Stop
Command : EITHER service mosmb stop OR systemctl stop mosmb Output  : Stopping MoSMB service...

When MoSMB is being used with ZooKeeper server, the sequence of stopping these services / shutting down the machines is important as MoSMB is ZooKeeper client and ZooKeeper takes the locks on certain files which is released after 200 secs timeout if the MoSMB / ZooKeeper server is exited abruptly or MoSMB / ZooKeeper server machine is rebooted.

1. Stop MoSMB service Command : EITHER service mosmb stop OR systemctl stop mosmb Output  : Stopping MoSMB service... 2. Stop ZooKeeper service/s Command : apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkServer.sh stop Output  : .... Stopping zookeeper ... STOPPED

When MoSMB is being used with ZooKeeper server, the sequence of stopping these services / shutting down the machines is important as MoSMB is ZooKeeper client and ZooKeeper takes the locks on certain files which is released after 200 secs timeout if the MoSMB / ZooKeeper server is exited abruptly or MoSMB / ZooKeeper server machine is rebooted.

1. Stop MoSMB service on all nodes - Command : EITHER service mosmb stop OR systemctl stop mosmb Output  : Stopping MoSMB service... 2. Stop ZooKeeper service on all nodes - Command : apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkServer.sh stop Output  : .... Stopping zookeeper ... STOPPED
MoSMB Restart
MoSMB service restart is mandatory in case any changes are made to the configuration file.
Command : EITHER service mosmb restart OR systemctl restart mosmb Output  : Stopping MoSMB service... Starting MoSMB service...

In case MoSMB goes in an unstable state due to node/process/network failures causing MoSMB and/or zookeeper node/s to go down or unreachable within recovery time periods, then follow the steps as mentioned below for clean restart the MoSMB along with MoSMB-ZooKeeper.

1. Stop MoSMB - Command : EITHER service mosmb stop OR systemctl stop mosmb 2. Run /etc/mosmb/scripts/zk-cleanup.sh script to clear the zookeeper database and logs on zookeeper node. ( If zookeeper is not on the MoSMB node, in that case manually delete all the files in <zookeeper-datadir>/version-2 and <zookeeper-install-dir>/logs ) /etc/mosmb/scripts/zk-cleanup.sh <zookeper-install-dir> 3. Stop zookeeper <zookeeper-install-dir>/bin/zkServer.sh stop 4. Start zookeeper <zookeeper-install-dir>/bin/zkServer.sh start 5. Start MoSMB service mosmb start

In case MoSMB cluster goes in an unstable state due to node/process/network failures causing multiple MoSMB and/or zookeeper nodes to go down or unreachable within recovery time periods, then follow the steps as mentioned below for clean restart the whole MoSMB cluster along with MoSMB-ZooKeeper cluster.

1. Stop MoSMB cluster (mosmb service on all nodes) Command : EITHER service mosmb stop OR systemctl stop mosmb 2. Delete all mo_openfiletable files from pstore path
   3. Stop zookeeper cluster (zookeeper on all nodes) <zookeeper-install-dir>/bin/zkServer.sh stop 4. Run the zookeeper cleanup script by MoSMB to clear the zookeeper database and logs on all zookeeper nodes in the cluster. <zk-cleanup script> <zookeper-install-dir path> /etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh <zookeper-install-dir path> (For Release versions MoSMB : 3.1.1.005 onwards) /etc/mosmb/scripts/zk-cleanup.sh <zookeper-install-dir path> (For Release versions MoSMB : 3.1.1.003 and before) ( If zookeeper is not on the MoSMB node, in that case manually delete all the files in <zookeeper-datadir>/version-2 and <zookeeper-install-dir>/logs OR in a workaround copy the clean up script to mosmb-zookeeper node and run it from there.)   
   
   5. Start zookeeper cluster (zookeeper on all nodes) <zookeeper-install-dir>/bin/zkServer.sh start 6. Start MoSMB cluster (mosmb service on all nodes) service mosmb start
MoSMB Status
Command : EITHER service mosmb status OR systemctl status mosmb Output  : MoSMB service is running OR MoSMB service is NOT running

Command : ps aux | grep mosmb OR pidof mosmb Output  : root      7521  4.7  4.5 1646804 272104 pts/1  Sl   14:31   0:00 mosmb root      7534  0.0  0.0 112708   976 pts/1    S+   14:31   0:00 grep --color=auto mosmb
MoSMB Logs

Once you start the MoSMB service, check the mosmb.log located at default path /var/log/ to see if MoSMB has started successfully without any errors. For any errors, check the Troubleshooting Guide or FAQs for assistance. Logs directory can be configured as per the requirement by editing parameter logsPath in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json . In logs directory MoSMB will create files mosmb.log, mosmb_audit.log, mosmb_vinode.log Please send your queries to support@ryussi.com
For mosmb logs, the log level values for all modules can be set in the conf file /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf
MoSMB Logs in release versions 3.1.1.039 and above
MO_CRITICAL = 0
MO_ERROR = 1
MO_WARN = 2
MO_INFO = 3
MO_DEBUG = 4
Default mosmb_logging.conf looks like

FSA_L           = 2
SMBCORE_L       = 2
CONNECTION_L    = 2
MULTICHANNEL_L  = 2
IOS_L           = 2
RDMA_L          = 2
WITNESS_L       = 2
OPLOCKS_L       = 2
MORPC_L         = 2
CONFIG_L        = 3
STATISTICS_L    = 2
LEASE_L         = 2
NDR_L           = 2
RPC_L           = 2
HASH_L          = 2
PST_L           = 2
LICENSE_L       = 2
AUDIT_L         = 2
AUTH_L          = 2
ENCRYPTION_L    = 2
VINODE_L        = 2
DEFAULT_L       = 3
COMMON_L        = 2
CLUSTER_L       = 2
PROFILER_L      = 2
The MO_CRITICAL log messages are printed with loglevel set to 0
(They will be printed for all other log level > 0 as well)
When the log level value set is to 1, error level logs are enabled (MO_ERROR Level) (They will be printed for all other log level > 1 as well)
The MO_WARN log messages are printed with log level set to 2 in the conf file.
(They will be printed for all other log level > 2 as well)

For modules CONFIG_L and DEFAULT_L, default value is set to 3, which is MO_INFO. The INFO logs are printed to give more information. You can choose to disable it and print only error level logs by setting the value to < 3
For module PROFILER_L, default is set to 0. When the value is set to 4, the detailed information is printed.
Any changes to log levels can be reloaded at runtime, (no need to restart mosmb) reload Log Level Changes
In case debug level is required, following guidelines can be used to set respective log modules to debug level

Login/Access issues- enable AUDIT_L , AUTH_L, SMBCORE_L
File I/O issues - enable IOS_L, FSA_L, SMBCORE_L
Connection related issues - enable CONNECTION_L , SMBCORE_L
Overall general profiling for timings - enable PROFILER_L
RPC failures- enable - RPC_L, SMBCORE_L
mosmb-zookeeper related issues- VINODE_L, SMBCORE_L

Use these logs for Security Monitoring.
One can set the audit module’s log level in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file to 3 to get detailed audit logs.

AUDIT_L = 3
This level logs File operations like CREATE RENAME DELETE Or PERMISSION CHANGE With FileName and the User who is attempting the operation
Note: Logs for MO_CRIT, MO_ERROR, MO_WARN will also be included
If MO_WARN level logs are required, one can set

AUDIT_L = 2
This level logs LOGIN and LOGOFF attempts, success or failure, With Time and IP of machine the attempt was made from
The mosmb_audit.log audit log file will then be generated at the log location (default  /var/log). Further details are same as older releases.

These are the logs generated by the api used to communicate to the (zookeeper/inode) database.
In case of any vinode related errors (either inode Mechanism or zookeeper), user can refer to these logs to get more details. The vinode loglevel starts from 1. For value 0 you may get error and mosmb will not start.

VINODE_L = 1
  Or 2/3/4 etc
The mosmb_vinode.log log file is populated at the log location, with MO_ERROR,MO_WARN,MO_INFO (Rest could be the Zookeeper logs.)
MoSMB Logs in older releases
MO_CRITICAL = -1
MO_ERROR = 0
MO_WARN = 1
MO_INFO = 2
MO_DEBUG = 3
The default mosmb_logging.conf looks like

Sample output of critical log in mosmb.log:
[2023-02-28 11:16:54.357]:[MO_CRITICAL]:[SMBCORE]:[Thread 4876]: Stopping mosmb as mosmb-zookeeper has got disconnected. Please check mosmb-zookeeper service availability. pid : 4849
When the log level value set is to 0, only error level logs are enabled (MO_ERROR Level) (They will be printed for all other log level > 0 as well)
The MO_WARN log messages are printed with log level set to 1 in the conf file.

MoSMB Logs rotation
When MoSMB is installed, the scripts for the log rotation of the mosmb logs are added in the /etc/cron.hourly directory (mologrotate) and the other files are in /etc/logrotate.d directory.
/etc/logrotate.d/mosmbauditlr
/etc/logrotate.d/mosmblr
/etc/logrotate.d/zookeeperlr
Logs are rotated when the log file size reaches 500 MB(default). The log size threshold reached is checked on an hourly basis.
mosmb.log and other log files are rotated nd refer to files mosmbauditlr (mosmb_audit.log), mosmblr (mosmb.log) and zookeeperlr (mosmb_vinode.log) present under /etc/logrotate.d/ respectively
Adding a share dynamically

Script: cmdpipe Function:
To add a new share to MoSMB server and reload the configuration without stopping the MoSMB server. Only single share can be applied at a time on reload command. To add multiple shares, add all the share names to mosmb.json under shareSection. Run the script to add each share one at a time. Reload MoSMB config will take some time. The newly added share's shareName should be unique. sharePath must be valid and must exists on the server. Before running the command, please make sure the mosmb.json is correct.
Prerequisite to run cmdpipe:
python2 should be installed.
tabulate module should be installed.
To install tabulate :
pip2 install tabulate

Example: To add a share without restarting the mosmb server 1. Add required VFS share details in mosmb.json under shareSection as a new share e.g. ‘share3' as follows. ’share1’ is an existing share. 2. Add required S3 share details in mosmb.json under shareSection as a new share e.g. ‘s3_share' as follows. ’share1’ is an existing share. 3. The command mentioned below will add share3 and reload MoSMB server configuration [root@sivm31 ~]# /etc/mosmb/cmdpipe config_reload_add -h Error occurred in GetOpt Use the following commands for mosmb config reload -n : Specifies share name -t : Specifies share type Share type can be one of these two : VFS or S3
Following is an Example: ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n <share_name> -t vfs OR ./cmdpipe config_reload_add -n <share_name> -t S3

[root@sivm31 ~]# /etc/mosmb/cmdpipe config_reload_add -n share3 -t vfs 
Success
Command executed successfully

[root@sivm31 ~]# /etc/mosmb/cmdpipe config_reload_add -n s3_share -t S3 
Success
Command executed successfully
MoSMB statistics
MoSMB statistics can be collected anytime with the command pipe utility
/etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe statson
Optional Arguments to statson

-p, --path Specifies csv path. Default is /var/log/
-i, --interval Specifies time period in seconds. Default is 300 s
-c, --console Display statistics on 'console'
-n, --number Number of counts for statistics collection operation
-h, --help Shows this help message and Exit
Examples:

/etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 60 & => Statistic collection will keep running in background untill mosmb is restarted or cmdpipe instance is stopped /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 120 -c => Display the stats on console after every '-i' interval /etc/mosmb/scripts/cmdpipe statson -p /var/log/ -i 600 -c -n 5 => Display the stats on console after every '-i' interval for '-n' number of time
Following Abbreviations used while showing output on console using '-c' option

RIO(Read IOPS), WIO(Write IOPS), TIOPs(Total IOPS), RT(Read Throughput), WT(Write Throughput),
TT(MB/s)(Total Throughput MB/s), RL(Read Latency), WL(Write Latency), SRL(Storage Read Latency),
SWL(Storage Write Latency), FO(Total File/Pipe Opened), ES(Total Established Sessions),
DC(Total Disconnects), PV(Password Violations), PE(Total Permission Error)
When statistics are collected in csv files, following files get populated in the default path or the path mentioned in command with –p

1. preprocessor_stats.csv
2. server_stats.csv
3. worker_thread_stats.csv
4. smbdirect_stats.csv
5. processed_stats.csv
User can use the processed_stats.csv file to check the collected and processed statistics
processed_stats.csv can be used to integrate with monitoring tools to visualizing and monitoring the metrics
Performance Tuning
Set the mosmb log level to 0 (zero) in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file.

Set the Required Message Signing to Off to improve the performance. This will not disable the signing completely: When the client sends a signed request the MoSMB server will send the signed response. For more information refer to Microsoft Network Server: Digitally Sign Communications

You can switch on multichannel by setting Enable MultiChannel to yes in the MoSMB configuration. Multiple network cards can be added at the server end. In case there are multiple network paths between the client and the server, the SMB3 protocol uses the multi-channel feature.
MoSMB is pre-tuned to perform well for most of the workloads. The MoSMB performance scales linearly with smb connections.
MoSMB service restart is mandatory in case any changes are made to the configuration file.

One can use the performance tuning script provided by MoSMB at location /etc/mosmb/scripts to apply suggested parameters w.r.t server machines CPU and RAM. To run for smbd parameters tuning ---- sh /etc/mosmb/scripts/perf_tuning.sh smbdirect To run for tcp parameters tuning ---- sh /etc/mosmb/scripts/perf_tuning.sh tcp Detailed usage is provided in script ---- sh /etc/mosmb/scripts/perf_tuning.sh -h
TCP Performance
In the MoSMB configuration, set all the transaction sizes to 8M. (Max Transaction Size, Max Read Size, Max Write Size)
Memory allocation is shared in all the TCP connections and Memory utilization is dependent on the following formula: preProcessorThreads * ringBufferCount * Transaction size

Ways to scale-up and scale-out MoSMB performance: i. In case the client connections are more in number, increase the server threads or pre-processing threads. ii. The number of threads in the worker thread-pool can be increased so that more number of SMB packets can be served at a time. The number of threads can be increased to 64/128/256 for a high-end system, depending on the CPU cores of the system. iii. The number of pre-allocated buffers can be increased as per the RAM memory. iv. Add an additional network card to make the multi-channel feature functional and enable the multi-channel settings in the MoSMB configuration. v. To scale up the MoSMB on one node, add more NICs, CPU or memory as desired. If the backend storage is a bottleneck, add high performance storage like NVMe disks. vi. To scale out, add another MoSMB node to the cluster and expose the same share.

Note : MoSMB provides a performance tuning script as per the server configuration so as MoSMB will use only upto  60% of the available memory on the server machine. This script is located at /etc/mosmb/scripts/perf_tuning.sh Just run the script to get tuning parameters like preProcessorThreads, threadPoolSize and ringBufferCount set as per the server configuration.
RDMA (SMBDirect) Performance
Note : Refer to the Troubleshooting Guide for the RDMA (SMBDirect) Communication on a MoSMB Server to understand how to make the RDMA (SMBDirect) basic setup work.

The SMBDirect protocol uses the RDMA Read/Write requests to transfer file data within the client and the server. Every RDMA Read/Write operation consists of a SMB2 Read request with 1 or more buffer descriptors . Each buffer descriptor contains information for an I/O buffer with the desired transfer size. Each buffer descriptor thus requires to be allocated and pinned in memory till the transfer is completed through the RDMA channel. So for every buffer descriptor a buffer of desired size is allocated and pinned, this is a control operation and consumes a significant amount of time. Therefore we have introduced the following parameters to ensure that we reduce control operations during data operations:

SMB Direct ring size: i. Default value: 512 ii. Per connection based parameter. iii. Accepts value in the multiples of 64. iv. The server should be restarted to reflect the effect when a  parameter is changed. The SMB Direct ring size is a parameter that allocates and reserves the specified number of buffers, each of the SMBDirect buffer size size. On every RDMA Read/Write request the buffer is consumed from this ring and replenished after use. This allows faster operations as we can avoid memory pinning operations (control) in the data path resulting in faster speeds. This can to be tuned w.r.t the I/O pattern for typical application. Setting it to very higher number can guarantee faster transfers at the cost of higher memory footprint. Whereas setting it to very lower number can lead to slower speeds due to the lack of available buffers for transfers. We recommend it to be set 1024 for media applications, and 256 for non-media operations (basic file server, disk io apps)

SMB Direct buffer size: i. Default value: 1 ii. Per connection-based parameter. iii. Accepts value from 1 to 8 inclusive. This value is in Mega Bytes (for example 1 represents 1MB). iv. The server should be restarted to reflect the effect of a changed parameter. This parameter represents the size of transfer for every buffer descriptor from SMB Direct ring . As per the SMBD Specification this parameter should be less than or equal to the maximum fragmented receive size (1MB). However, this parameter could be less than or equal to the maximum transaction size (8MB) as well. The SMB client has 1MB value  that is the maximum fragmented receive size and hence it the recommended value.

TCP parameters i. For RDMA connections only, allocate minimal resources to TCP parameters since the TCP buffers will consume some amount of RAM ii. For example, preProcessorThreads = 4, ringBufferCount = 256 (or 128 or 64)

The file /opt/mapr/conf/warden.conf controls parameters related to MapR services and the Warden. Any change made to warden.conf will require following procedure to be followed
1. Check Node's role
maprcli node list -columns svc,ip
2. If it has CLDB, Stop the CLDB
maprcli node services -name cldb -action stop -nodes <hostname>
3. Put the node in maintenance mode.
maprcli node maintenance -nodes <hostname> -timeoutminutes 60
4. Stop Warden service on this node as root.
systemctl stop mapr-warden
5. Stop Zookeeper service on this node as root if running.
systemctl stop mapr-zookeeper
6. Check that all services are stopped on this node
jps
7. change the required parameter in /opt/mapr/conf/warden.conf 8. Run the configure.sh file on this node.
/opt/mapr/server/configure.sh -R
9. Start ZooKeeper service if ZooKeeper is installed on the node.
systemctl start mapr-zookeeper
10. Start Warden service on this node.
systemctl start mapr-warden
10. Bring the node out of maintenance mode.
maprcli node maintenance -nodes <hostname> -timeoutminutes 0

11. Wait for the cldb to start and the containers to synchronize, if this node is running CLDB service.
Check the status of CLDB service.
maprcli dump cldbstate
12. Verify that the data is synchronized. No output signifies that the containers are synchronized.
/opt/mapr/server/mrconfig info containers resync local

Permission mapping Windows to Linux

In the Windows world, permissions are more granular than Linux. Each file system object is associated with multiple users/groups. Each of which may contain around fourteen/fifteen permissions. When file object is accessed by both the world Linux and Windows then to maintain compatibility and consistency, mapping needs to be done. The mapping is challenging as in Linux only three permissions are defined vs fifteen in windows as seen below.
Setting Permissions On Linux
Extended permissions are set via setfacl command on linux side.

Example: Setting Extended ACEs - setfacl -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder> Setting Extended ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs - setfacl -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions>  <absolute path of file/folder>
Check extended/default permissions with getfacl command on linux side.
Example: getfacl <absolute path of file/folder>
Linux filesystem allows only Owner and superusers (sudo) to set or modify permissions of files and folders.
In Linux world “owner“, “primary group” and “other” can not be removed, though all permissions can be removed for the same except for the owner. Read permissions for the owner can not be removed.
Example: file/folder can have Owner:Group:Other permissions to any combination of rwx but lowest it can be set to as r--:---:---
If the parent has execute permissions for users children of that parent will be enumerated; otherwise they won't be.
Inheritance
All files and folders in the share have Inheritance enabled by default.
All inherited permissions are by default applied as This folder subfolder and files .
Permissions will be inherited on basis of applies to section mentioned while setting new permissions or inheriting existing as follows,
Rules and Limitations
On any share inheritance is always enabled. Disable inheritance button on windows client (Properties → security → Advance → Disable Inheritance) is not supported.
Only Allow ACE type is supported. Deny type from windows property tab is not supported.
Windows system ACL permission requests will not be supported.
Only Owner and super user is allowed to add new permissions, modify newly added permissions or remove newly added permissions on shares, files and folders.
Recommended to add permissions in default ACEs from server side on shared path for inheritance to be enabled.

When existing ACEs permissions on share root are modified; it will remove the inherited(default) permissions of that specific shared path. The user will be warned about this. This behavior is in adherence to Windows smb share.

Behavior of  checkbox - Only apply these permissions to objects and/or containers within this container - When any new user/group permission is added with this checkbox selected then it will be applied as "This folder only" for the current folder and to it's existing immediate children as "This folder only"

Special case - EVERYONE SID We give inherited permissions higher priority over Everyone when Everyone has lower permissions than inherited permissions then only inherited permissions will be considered.

It is not recommended to mix linux local users or root with AD users/groups in permissions - Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display. When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux.
Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display.

When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux.
Access time mask or acl mask is to be kept as rwx always (mask::rwx & default:mask::rwx), as permissions seen on windows client side are not filtered.

Creation time mask or mosmb mask (fileCreationMask & directoryCreationMask) can be kept as per the need. Please not that In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx
In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx
In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx
Advance Posix ACL module supports multiple group mapping for any user up to 256 groups.

How to Create HyperV VM
HyperV VM creation is currently only supported in the krb5 authentication mode and the posix acl authorization mode configuration. Verify if all of the following settings are done:
The MoSMB share must have the complete permissions set for all. (777 -- drwxrwxrwx)
The MoSMB share Ownership Settings must be carried out.
The File Creation Mask and Directory Creation Mask are set to 0777 in the MoSMB Share Configuration .
Sign in to the HyperV machine with the Domain Administrator user credentials.
The HyperV machine's primary group must be the same as the MoSMB share owner and it must be the Domain Admin.

Note : The Domain Admin requirement depends on the HyperV configuration settings. If a non-admin user has permissions to create the HyperV VM then this user can create the VM on the MoSMB share - if the share settings are configured for that non-admin user.
How to do Performance Tuning for MoSMB

To scale up the MoSMB on one node, add more NICs, CPU or memory as desired. If the backend storage is a bottleneck, add high performance storage like NVMe disks. To scale out, add another MoSMB node to the cluster and expose the same share.
For detailed information please refer Performance Tuning pages

Note: For TCP For a given NIC, set minimum values if you want MoSMB to use low RAM and CPU cores For a given NIC, set maximum values if you want MoSMB to use higher RAM and CPU cores For a given NIC - Keep the TCP buffers:thread ratio approximately as given in the above column for e.g. if you increase the no of threads by 1 for 10Gbps bw then increase the ringbufferCount by 8 (ringbufferCount × preprocessor threads 8 × 8=64 TCP buffers) Set TransactionSize=8 for large files transactions Set TransactionSize=1 for small file io transactions
Note: For RDMA + TCP threadPoolSize is common to both RDMA and TCP transport.
How to use MoSMB Audit Logs for Security Monitoring
Use these logs for Security Monitoring. One can set the audit module’s log level in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file to 2 to get detailed audit logs .
Set the audit log level in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file to 3. AUDIT_L = 3 Restart the MoSMB service OR update logging at runtime without restarting mosmb

The mosmb_audit.log audit log file will then be generated at the log location mentioned in the mosmb.json file, default is /var/log . Audit logs are populated for following categories: CREATE RENAME DELETE PERMISSION_CHANGE LOGIN LOGOFF

When MoSMB is installed, the scripts for the log rotation of the mosmb audit logs are added in the /etc/cron.hourly directory (mologrotate) and the /etc/logrotate.d directory. Logs are rotated when the log file size reaches 500 MB. The log size threshold reached is checked on an hourly basis.
To disable the audit logs set the audit log level to 0. (ERROR level) AUDIT_L = 0 Restart the MoSMB service OR update logging at runtime without restarting mosmb

Note that : Along with mosmb_audit.log other log files are rotated in similar way; with files mosmbauditlr (mosmb_audit.log), mosmblr (mosmb.log) and zookeeperlr (mosmb_vinode.log) present under /etc/logrotate.d/

As part of strict json syntax handling while running the MoSMB server, the user should ensure that - 1. Opening or closing curly braces for the entire json file should not be missing. 2. All keys and values should be separated by : 3. Comments in mosmb.json are not allowed  currently.

RC4 encryption deprecated in CentOS 8.*
In CentOS 8.* , RC4 encryption is disabled in the DEFAULT crypto policy level.
Following changes are needed for kerberos to work on CentOS 8*
There are two solutions (To maintain stronger security, we suggest you use first option)
1. Changes in User's Properties in the AD

In AD, go to Users and Computers -> Users ->Select the special user-> Open
properties ->select account tab, and choose the following account options
This account supports Kerberos AES 128 bit encryption
This account supports Kerberos AES 256 bit encryption
OR
2. On mosmb server make changes to DEFAULT crypto policy, to include RC4
i) On every CentOS 8.3 and above mosmb server, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place,
Use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

[root@host ~]# update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT
       Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT
       Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up.
       It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies
       to fully take place.
Restart the host.
IMPORTANT
The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on CentOS 8.3 and newer.

ii) To enable support for RC4 in CentOS 8.2 , create and enable a custom cryptographic module policy with cipher = RC4-128+. For more details, see Customizing system-wide cryptographic policies with policy modifiers.

https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/8/html/security_hardening/using-the-system-wide-cryptographic-policies_security-hardening#customizing-system-wide-cryptographic-policies-with-policy-modifiers_using-the-system-wide-cryptographic-policies
https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/how-customize-crypto-policies-rhel-82?extIdCarryOver=true&sc_cid=701f2000001OH7JAAW
iii) To enable support for RC4 in CentOS 8.0 and CentOS 8.1 , add +rc4 to the permitted_enctypes option in the /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/krb5.config file

[libdefaults]
       permitted_enctypes = aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192 camellia256-cts-cmac aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128 camellia128-cts-cmac +rc4
What if I want to change the node_id for some reason

In a situation, where one wants to change the node_id of a node, from a cluster, it is recommended that full cluster is stopped (MoSMB and Zookeeper both) and a clean up is carried out, before you change the node_id of a node or many nodes in the mosmb.json.

1. stop mosmb service on all nodes
2. stop mosmb-zookeeper on all zookeeper nodes
3. run the clean up command
/etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh <zookeeper install directory path>
e.g. /etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin 4. once the clean up is finished, open /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json of the node where you want to change the node_id, and update it. (one node or many nodes of cluster as per your requirement)
5. Save the json files and restart the cluster, first all zk servers and then all mosmb services in the cluster nodes

mosmbcli
mosmbcli is command line parser. mosmbcli binary is made available in /usr/bin after MoSMB installation. mosmbcli can be used for various purposes like,
To generate or update the MoSMB configuration in json format.
To manage mosmb services on local/standalone setups.
To manage mosmb services on cluster setups.
Prerequisites
Python and pip of same version.
Command-line JSON processor : jq package
zk-shell

Setup passwordless SSH : ssh-keygen -t rsa ssh-copy-id <hostname_of_other_mosmb_server> Allow user to run any commands anywhere without password - edit /etc/sudoers - <username> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL For more reference see : passwordless-ssh
ssh-keygen -t rsa
ssh-copy-id <hostname_of_other_mosmb_server>
Allow user to run any commands anywhere without password - edit /etc/sudoers - <username> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL
For more reference see : passwordless-ssh

Clustershell aka clush configuration is required for mosmbcli to work. Installing clustershell After installation open the file /etc/clustershell/groups.d/local.cfg and make changes to add the required mosmb_server_nodes. Example: all: host1 If in mosmb cluster mosmb node's hostnames are mohost1, mohost2, mohost3, then one can add a group as group1: mohost[1,2,3] Note: groups in /etc/clustershell/groups.d/local.cfg, should contain nodes having only hostnames. As username@ip will not work

                       {zk-start,zk-restart,zk-stop,zk-status,mosmb-start,mosmb-restart,mosmb-stop,mosmb-status,get-req,deploy-resp,znode,get,set,del,validate,apply}
                       ...

         {zk-start,zk-restart,zk-stop,zk-status,mosmb-start,mosmb-restart,mosmb-stop,mosmb-status,get-req,deploy-resp,znode,get,set,del,validate,apply}

To get help for each command use "-h" or "--help" at each level of command :
Example :

1) mosmbcli -h

MoSMB
For MoSMB we have start, restart, stop and check status options supported with local or remote nodes; similar to mosmb-zookeeper.
Create a /etc/mosmb/mosmb-clustermap.txt and add entries in following format:

NodeId Machine-IP username Password 1 88.88.88.88 root Pa55w0rd
   2 88.88.88.89 root Pa55w0rd

MoSMB License

For MoSMB Offline License activation we have get-req which generates the mosmb offline license requests on the mosmb cluster nodes and deploy-resp which deploys the shared response file for mosmb offline license activation on the mosmb cluster nodes.
One needs MoSMB Product Key in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file;

Request and response will be created against mentioned product key in /etc/msomb/mosmb.json Once request's for all mentioned nodes are created then share the tar file with mosmb support and you will receive the corresponding response. Use deploy-resp command to deploy the responses on corresponding machines at required location.

MoSMB json Manipulation

One can use the get, set, validate and apply functions to the manipulate the MoSMB configurations in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json Standard/Default path for the json file is /etc/mosmb/ . If user of "mosmbcli" wants to redirect the output files to particular location, then the environment variable MOSMB_CONF_PATH has to be set with path of that location. Eg. export MOSMB_CONF_PATH=/home/user Note that mosmb.json generated at exported path won't get applied on MoSMB service start/restart
Command-line JSON processor : jq package

This is an introduction to MoSMB to get you started with the MoSMB server on any server platform like CentOS, Ubuntu, and Debian.

MoSMB is a proprietary ANSI C SMB v3 Server implementation in user space only and supported on major Linux flavors. It is designed to be fully compliant with Microsoft Server Message Block (SMB) version 3 Protocol specifications. It is designed to be a high performance, scalable, highly available, user-space server implementation that has an API for third-party integration.
The entire MoSMB configuration can be categorized into seven major categories, namely:
Server Modes
Authentication Modes
Authorization Modes
Networking (DNS & NTP)
MoSMB License settings
Share Configuration , and
Maintenance
MoSMB Features
The following MoSMB features are available in the various MoSMB versions - Standard, Advance and Media.
Major Features :
Microsoft SMB 2.* and 3.0.* Protocol support
Support for VFS filesystems (Ext4, XFS, ZFS, GlusterFS, LustreFS, and so on)
Active Directory Integration and Kerberos support
NTLMSSP (NTLM-AD) support
NTLM local support
Signing with HMAC-SHA256, AES-CMAC, AES-GMAC ciphers
Encryption with AES-128-CCM, AES-128-GCM, AES-256-CCM, AES-256-GCM ciphers
SMB Direct (RDMA) support
RDMA data transformation using the above mentioned signing and encryption ciphers
Basic Posix ACLs support
Basic Windows ACLs (DACLs only)
Advance Posix ACL (using extended attributes)
SMB multi-channel
Persistent Handle v1 and v2
SMB open file modes, SMB locks supported across cluster nodes
Active-Passive cluster support
Active-Active Scale-out cluster support
Highly Salable as per system resources available (no of connections, no of sessions, no of simultaneous file opens etc.)
Transparent Failover and High Availability support
Distributed MoSMB file shares as Distributed File System
SMB clients supported – Windows, Mac, Linux
High Performance (iops/latency/throughput)
Server Side Copy Operation Support
Direct I/O support for RDMA (and TCP)
Low Resource Usage (CPU/memory/etc)
MoSMB supports S3 Object Store interface (AddOn: S3 plugin)
S3 driver plug-in for Object Stores like AWS, Minio, and so on
Compatible with S3 browsers
Basic MMC support for management
Config CLI support for MoSMB configuration (mosmbcli)
Microsoft SMB 3.1.1 Protocol Compliance
Performance Metrices are available
MoSMB metrices Integration support available for monitoring tools like Checkmk, Prometheus
Multi domain, multi forest support
File and directory Leasing
Named Streams
AAPL Context
Ansible scripts for installation and upgrade
Witness Server
Stale file handling and scavenging
SMB Tree Connect Redirection (Load Balancing using storage node affinity)
Minor Features :
Provisioning of shares at runtime
Unicode support
DFS shares Compatible
Heterogeneous storage support at share level
Support for LDAPS communication
Upcoming Features :
NFSv4 ACL support
S3 Bucket Notifications
Supported Use Cases
MS SQL 2012 and above databases hosted on the MoSMB SMB3 shares.
Hyper-V (managed by SCVMM). VM stored on the MoSMB SMB3 shares. Use cases such as VM Cloning, Template, Live Migration, and so on.
High performance File Server for User Home Directory and Media Streaming.
Continuously Available (CA) for Client Applications using the Witness Server.
System Requirements
and Network Time Protocol Service (NTP), LDAP, SSSD
Port List for MoSMB
The following is a comprehensive list of port numbers used by the MoSMB server and MoSMB related miscellaneous services:

From version 091 onwards, MoSMB will work with LDAPS. MoSMB will attempt Secure LDAP (LDAP over SSL/TLS) communication to the AD server (to port 636) and if it is not setup then will fallback to non secure LDAP communication over port 389. The port 636 communication setup to AD can be checked with command openssl s_client -connect <AD_IP>:636
Supported SMB Clients
Multi-protocol Caveat

MoSMB does NOT recommend and support concurrent multiprotocol write access of the same file or directory via SMB and NFS/native posix client/backend access. This is because mosmb supports distributed SMB locking, byte-range locks, file sharing modes etc. and other protocols do not. Concurrent access over other protocols (or natively) and modifying files/folders without unified locking can cause data inconsistency for Windows applications that are accessing over SMB (mosmb).

Background and Recommendation In an enterprise environment, it is common that Linux, Windows and macOS workloads require access to the same data. It is recommended that multiprotocol access NOT be used for this purpose. Instead, either MoSMB or NFS, but not both should be used to access a particular file. This is because MoSMB and NFS use different locking mechanisms and using both to access a file concurrently can lead to data inconsistency issues. That is why, for example, Amazon EFS, Amazon FSx, or Azure Files, provide access to files via either NFS or SMB, not both. Since it is not possible to control access at the granularity of a single file or directory, it is recommended that data should be partitioned at the volume level so that each volume is accessed via NFS or MoSMB, but not both. MoSMB supports access to all 3- Linux, Windows and macOS clients in a consistent manner, so there should be no data consistency issue when these different OS clients access the same file or directory concurrently.

The above data consistency issue can also occur when accessing files concurrently via MoSMB and natively using the POSIX client. It is recommended that for such access also only one of the access mechanisms be used for a particular volume and not both.
POSIX ACL is Portable Operating System Interface - Access Control Lists.
Note : POSIX ACL for MoSMB means only the Server user's ID mapping to the MoSMB share and its included files and directories.
MoSMB Configuration
For the POSIX ACL view  MoSMB share configurations are required as well.
"aclStrategy":{ "acl": "posix", "checkPosixPermissionBit": "yes" },
In case of posix ACL and ntlm_local authentication you are required to:
Set the permissions of the share path to the Linux local user.
Add the local linux user as a MoSMB user using the mosmb_passwd binary. Refer to ntlm_local Authentication for details.
The ownership of the share path should be set to the Linux user and the user group must be set as the MoSMB user.
sudo chown -R <linux-user-name> : <linux-group-name> <share path>
Example :
Test Data : motest, motest, /opt/share Command   : sudo chown -R motest:motest /opt/share
The user name and group will be displayed on the client side as follows:
Important : Kindly complete the Network Configuration Settings before you configure Kerberos on the MoSMB server.
